title: Multi-tenant log viewer with virtualized stream + inline schema introspection
design_brief: You're building the runtime logs view for a serverless platform (think Vercel Functions + Datadog Live Tail). Engineers stare at this during incidents. Pain point: existing log viewers (Cloudwatch, Papertrail) either dump a wall of text or hide structure behind expand-collapse chevrons. The bar: Vercel's runtime logs, Datadog Live Tail, Axiom. The viewer must handle 5k log lines/sec without dropping a frame and let engineers pivot from a raw line into structured exploration in a single keystroke.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Rendering every log line as a div in a scrollable container (will die at 200 lines)
Using a generic <Table> with expand chevrons for structured payloads
Color-coding severity with bg-red-500 / bg-yellow-500 — looks like a 2014 admin panel
Auto-scroll that fights the user when they scroll up to read
JSON.stringify(payload, null, 2) inside a <pre> as the 'structured view'
ux_reasoning:
Three competing pressures: throughput (5k lines/sec), scannability (severity + timestamp + service must be readable at a glance), and depth (when an error matters, the engineer needs the full structured payload immediately). The interaction model is borrowed from `less +F` and Datadog: when the user is pinned to the bottom, new lines stream in; the moment they scroll up, streaming pauses with a subtle 'NEW LINES (243)' floating pill at the bottom — clicking it OR pressing G re-pins.

Density comes from three decisions. First, monospace for the log body but a proportional sans for the chrome (timestamp gutter, service badge) — this is Vercel's move and it doubles perceived information without crowding. Second, severity is encoded as a 2px left border, not a background fill — color screams from a tiny stripe instead of bleeding into the text. Third, structured payloads are inline-flattened: instead of {user_id: 'abc'} as JSON, we render `user_id=abc` as faint key=value chips that the user can Cmd+click to add as a filter (Splunk's killer move, but with Linear's chip aesthetic).

Virtualization is non-negotiable. We use a windowed list with variable row heights cached in a Map keyed by log_id. The hard part: log lines can wrap, and wrap height depends on container width. We measure on first paint via ResizeObserver and cache; on container resize we invalidate the cache for visible rows only and let off-screen rows re-measure lazily. Rejected: react-window's FixedSizeList (kills the design — long lines need to wrap, not truncate with an ellipsis that hides the bug you're hunting).

Motion: zero spring animations. New lines appear instantly. The only motion is the 'NEW LINES' pill, which fades in over 120ms with a linear ease. Engineers find spring physics in a log viewer infuriating during an incident — Linear's lesson.

Failure modes: stream disconnect surfaces as a sticky banner at the top with the last successful timestamp + a retry countdown. Schema mismatch (payload not parseable as JSON) falls back to raw text without breaking the row.

design_tokens.colors: Severity stripes: --sev-error: oklch(0.62 0.21 25); --sev-warn: oklch(0.78 0.16 75); --sev-info: oklch(0.55 0.02 250); --sev-debug: oklch(0.45 0.005 250). Background: --surface-0: oklch(0.16 0.005 250) dark / oklch(0.99 0.002 250) light. Text body: oklch(0.85 0.01 250) dark. Key chips: oklch(0.55 0.04 250) on transparent — readable but never competing with values.
design_tokens.typography: Body: 'Berkeley Mono', 'JetBrains Mono', monospace at 12.5px / 18px line-height (Vercel's exact log size). Chrome: 'Inter' at 11px tabular-nums for timestamps. Severity badge: 10px uppercase tracking-wider. Line numbers: tabular-nums, oklch(0.5 0.005 250).
design_tokens.spacing: Row vertical padding: 2px (extreme density). Gutter: 56px fixed for timestamp, 8px for severity stripe, 4px gap. Container max-width: none — logs should fill the viewport horizontally.
design_tokens.motion: Pill enter: 120ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1). Scroll-to-bottom: instant scrollTop assignment, never animated. Filter chip add: 80ms opacity fade only.

component_architecture: <LogStream<T extends LogRecord>> is the root — generic over the record shape so a serverless platform and a Kafka consumer can both use it. Children: <LogRow record={r} schema={schema} />, <LogVirtualizer items={records} estimatedSize={20} />, <StreamPin onResume={...} pendingCount={n} />. Schema is passed via React Context (LogSchemaProvider) because every row needs it but it never changes per-row — passing it as a prop would defeat memoization. Filters live in a separate FilterContext; clicking a chip dispatches an ADD_FILTER action.
interaction_details: Hover on a row: subtle background tint (not severity-colored — that would re-encode info already in the stripe). Cursor stays default unless over a pivotable key.
Cmd+click on a payload chip: adds it to the active filter set and shows a brief 80ms opacity pulse on the row. Plain click on a chip: text-selects the value so the engineer can copy.
First scroll up: streaming pauses, pill appears with count. After 10s of inactivity at scroll position: no nag, no auto-resume — engineers are reading.
Shift+G: jumps to bottom and re-pins. j/k: move row focus up/down without scrolling the page. Enter on focused row: expands full payload as JSON below the row.
Stream disconnect: row container desaturates 15% over 200ms, sticky banner appears at top with last-success timestamp and 'reconnecting in 3s' countdown.
accessibility_considerations: Rows use roving focus with j/k, Shift+G, Enter expansion, role=grid/row, aria-selected, and descriptive labels while virtualization keeps DOM size bounded.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Severity as a 2px stripe instead of a background fill — color screams without bleeding into the text. 2) Payload chips are inline key=value pairs that Cmd+click to filter, borrowing Splunk's pivot UX with Linear's chip taste. 3) Streaming pauses the moment the user scrolls up and resumes only on explicit Shift+G — no autoscroll wars during incidents.

title: Hierarchical command palette with mode stacking and inline previews
design_brief: You're building the command palette for a developer-facing analytics product (think PostHog + Linear). Power users live in this palette — they should rarely touch the mouse. Pain point: most palettes (cmd+k clones) are a flat fuzzy-searched list. Real workflows are hierarchical: 'go to project → switch env → open dashboard → filter by event'. The bar: Linear's palette (hierarchical, contextual, fast), Raycast (extension-driven, inline previews), Arc's command bar (mode-aware).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Flat fuzzy-search list with everything mashed together
Modal overlay that blurs the whole app and feels like leaving context
Showing 20+ results — users can't scan that many
Bouncy modal entrance with scale + opacity spring
Cmd+K to open and Esc to close is the only keyboard contract
Loading spinner inside the palette while async results load
ux_reasoning:
The palette is not a search box — it's a stack-based REPL for navigation. Each selection either executes immediately OR pushes a new mode onto the stack with its own context-scoped command set. Backspace at an empty input pops the stack (Linear's killer interaction). The user can build up 'project → production → events → checkout_completed' through pure keystrokes, and a breadcrumb above the input shows the stack so the user always knows where they are.

Fuzzy ranking is per-mode, not global. In the root mode, 'new' fuzzy-matches against 'New issue', 'New project', 'New dashboard'. In a project-scoped mode, 'new' matches against 'New dashboard in [project]'. This means the algorithm always has a tight relevance set — never the 800-command global pool.

Inline previews are the Raycast move. When the focused item is 'Open dashboard: Funnels', the right pane shows a 200px live preview of that dashboard, hydrated from cache. Cache miss: show the dashboard's metadata (last updated, owner, chart count) instead of a spinner — predicted information beats loading state.

Motion: the palette doesn't 'enter' — it appears in 80ms with an opacity fade only. No scale, no spring. The palette is a tool, not a moment. Mode transitions slide the result list horizontally by 8px with 120ms ease-out — just enough to communicate 'we went deeper' without choreography.

Rejected: a global command registry with tags. Tested it — fuzzy match across 800 commands ranks 'New issue' below 'Renew subscription' for query 'new'. Mode scoping kills this entire class of bug. Also rejected: a settings panel for customizing shortcuts. Power users will memorize whatever ships; configurability is a tax on the 95% to please the 5%.

design_tokens.colors: Surface: oklch(0.99 0.002 250 / 0.96) light, oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.96) dark, both with backdrop-blur. NEVER fully opaque — the palette should feel layered over the app. Selected row: oklch(0.94 0.01 250) light / oklch(0.24 0.01 250) dark — subtle, not a brand-color highlight. Mode chip: oklch(0.92 0.02 250) with oklch(0.4 0.04 250) text.
design_tokens.typography: Input: Inter 15px medium. Results: Inter 13px regular. Shortcut hints: 'JetBrains Mono' 11px in oklch(0.6 0.005 250). Mode breadcrumb: 11px with 4px gap, slash separators in 50% opacity.
design_tokens.spacing: Palette width: 640px fixed (Linear's exact number). Result row height: 36px. Padding: 12px input, 4px row vertical, 12px row horizontal. Preview pane: 280px right-side.
design_tokens.motion: Open: 80ms opacity-only fade. Mode push: 120ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) translateX(8px → 0). Selected row change: instant — never animated, would feel sluggish for j/k repetition.

component_architecture: <CommandPalette /> root holds the mode stack as state (PaletteMode[]). Each PaletteMode<TContext> is generic: { id, breadcrumb, getCommands: (ctx: TContext, query: string) => Command<TContext>[], context: TContext }. Commands are typed: type Command<T> = { id; label; shortcut?; preview?: ReactNode; execute: (ctx: T) => void | Promise<PaletteMode<unknown> | void> }. Async results from execute that return a new mode push it. <PreviewPane /> reads the focused command from CommandFocusContext.
interaction_details: Open: 80ms opacity fade, no scale, no spring. Closes on Esc or outside-click. Cmd+K toggles.
Arrow keys move focus, but hover ALSO moves focus — never two competing focus states. j/k as vim alternatives respected only when input is empty.
Backspace at empty input pops mode stack. Backspace at non-empty input deletes a character. This dual contract is invisible but learnable in 2 minutes.
Enter on a command that returns a new mode pushes; otherwise executes and closes. The user never has to know which is which — feedback is in the breadcrumb appearing.
Preview pane hydrates from cache. Cache miss: shows metadata (owner, last-updated, count) — never a spinner.
accessibility_considerations: role='dialog' with aria-label. Input has aria-controls pointing to the listbox and aria-activedescendant tracking focus — screen readers announce 'Open dashboard, Funnels, 3 of 12' as the user arrows. Listbox uses role='listbox' / role='option' / aria-selected pattern. Esc and outside-click close, with focus returned to the trigger via a focus restoration ref. Reduced-motion: opacity fade and mode-slide are disabled via @media query — the palette appears instantly. Color contrast: selected row is a luminance shift (not a brand color), so it survives high-contrast modes.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Stack-based modes with Backspace-to-pop — the palette becomes a navigable REPL, not a flat search. 2) Per-mode command scoping means fuzzy ranking is always tight and relevant. 3) Preview pane shows metadata on cache-miss instead of a spinner — predicted information beats loading state.

title: Multiplayer cursor presence with name-tag occlusion handling and idle decay
design_brief: You're building the presence layer for a collaborative whiteboard (think Figma + Miro + tldraw). Pain point: most multiplayer cursors render as a colored arrow with a name tag and that's it — fine for 2 users, claustrophobic at 8, broken at 20. Name tags overlap, idle cursors clutter, and when someone is selecting they need richer presence than a cursor dot. The bar: Figma's multiplayer (the gold standard), tldraw's cursor chat, Linear's presence chip in issue details.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Stacking name tags on top of each other when cursors are close — becomes unreadable
Keeping idle cursors at full opacity forever — they become visual debt
Animating cursor position with a 300ms spring — feels laggy, defeats the point
Rendering cursors as DOM elements that trigger layout on every move (use transform: translate3d only)
Showing every collaborator's selection rect at full saturation — the canvas becomes a Christmas tree
Generic 'User is typing' indicators with three bouncing dots
ux_reasoning:
Three problems to solve in concert: latency, density, and signal strength. Latency: cursor positions arrive over a websocket at ~30Hz. We interpolate between received positions with a linear lerp over the expected next-frame interval (33ms). No spring physics — they look 'nice' but introduce phantom lag that breaks the illusion of presence. This is Figma's exact choice.

Density: name tags collide. The solution is a quadtree-based occlusion pass that runs once per frame: for each visible cursor, check if its name-tag bbox overlaps another cursor's bbox. On collision, tags 'stack' vertically in a numbered list (think Apple's address book overlap), but only the focused/active cursor shows full name — others collapse to a colored dot with a count badge. Hovering the cluster expands them on a 200ms delay (slower than tooltip to avoid accidental triggers).

Idle decay: a cursor with no movement for 8s starts fading opacity (0.8 → 0.3 over 2s). At 30s of inactivity, the cursor collapses to a static avatar dot at the last position with a faint pulse on the next movement to draw the eye back. Returning users 'pop' back to full opacity in 200ms — never instant, never spring.

Selection presence: when a collaborator selects an object, we don't render a full-saturation outline. We render a 1px outline in their color at 60% opacity, plus a 12px name chip anchored to the selection's top-right. The chip is the load-bearing signal; the outline is supplementary. This is why Figma works at 20 collaborators — selection signal is information-dense but visually thin.

Rejected: Liveblocks-style 'presence cards' floating in a sidebar. They divorce identity from spatial context — the whole point of multiplayer cursors is that WHO is doing WHAT lives in the canvas. Also rejected: showing every cursor's click ripple. Once you have 6 users, ripples look like a fireworks display.

design_tokens.colors: Cursor colors are assigned from a curated 12-color palette (not random hue rotation): oklch values chosen for distinguishability under deuteranopia AND tritanopia. Example: --user-1: oklch(0.65 0.18 25); --user-2: oklch(0.7 0.16 145); --user-3: oklch(0.6 0.2 280). Tag bg: cursor color at L=0.95 (light bg, dark text) for tags. Selection outline: cursor color at 60% alpha.
design_tokens.typography: Name tags: Inter 11px medium, 12px line-height. Letter-spacing: 0 (no tracking — keep tags as visually compact as possible).
design_tokens.spacing: Cursor SVG: 16px hotspot. Tag: 6px horizontal padding, 2px vertical, 4px gap from cursor, anchored bottom-right of cursor. Cluster expansion: 4px vertical gap between stacked tags.
design_tokens.motion: Cursor position: linear lerp over 33ms (matching server tick). Tag fade on collision: 80ms opacity. Idle decay: 2000ms linear opacity ramp. Reactivation pop: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) — slight overshoot only on reactivation, NEVER on movement.

component_architecture: <PresenceLayer<TUser extends BaseUser>> generic over user shape. Uses requestAnimationFrame loop driven by a useRef<Map<userId, CursorState>>(). React render only triggers when the set of present users changes — actual position updates happen via direct DOM transform mutation on refs (the only sane way to hit 60fps with N cursors). <CursorTag /> is memoized per user-id. <OcclusionEngine /> is a non-rendering hook that runs the quadtree pass and updates a clustering Map that <CursorTag /> reads via a ref-based subscription (no rerender on cluster changes either).
interaction_details: Cursor movement: 33ms linear lerp toward target — never spring, never overshoot.
Two cursors collide: tags collapse, both show colored dots with a +N badge. Hovering the cluster for 200ms expands it.
Idle 8s: cursor begins fading opacity over 2s. Idle 30s: collapses to a static avatar dot.
Returning from idle: opacity pops back to 1.0 over 200ms with a subtle overshoot (the ONLY spring in the system) — communicates 'someone's back' without being loud.
Window blur (collaborator switched tabs): their cursor immediately drops to 0.4 opacity and shows a small zZz glyph next to the name. No fade — instant signal.
accessibility_considerations: aria-hidden='true' on the presence layer — cursors are decorative for sighted users only. Real presence info (who's here, what they're editing) lives in a separate <PresenceList /> sidebar that screen readers DO see, with aria-live='polite' for join/leave events. Color palette chosen to be distinguishable under deuteranopia and tritanopia (avoided pure red/green pairings). Reduced-motion: cursor positions jump rather than lerp (no interpolation), idle fades become instant transitions, reactivation pop becomes a simple opacity swap. Name tags use white text on saturated bg to maintain 4.5:1 contrast at the chosen lightness.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Cursor positions update via direct DOM transform mutation — React never rerenders during movement, so 20 cursors stay at 60fps. 2) Per-frame quadtree occlusion collapses overlapping tags into a count badge instead of stacking them — readable at any density. 3) Idle decay is 3-stage (full → 0.3 over 2s → static dot at 30s) so the canvas self-cleans without losing context.

title: Inbox archive gesture with progressive resistance and undo intent
design_brief: You're building the swipe-to-archive interaction for an email client (think Superhuman + Things 3 + Mail.app on iOS). Pain point: most swipe gestures are binary — swipe past threshold, action fires, toast appears with 'Undo'. The interaction has no relationship to the user's INTENT during the swipe. The bar: Things 3's drag-to-complete (the gold standard for kinetic feedback), Superhuman's swipe-to-archive, Mail.app's tiered swipe actions.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Linear translateX with a fixed threshold — swipe feels weightless
Snap-back animation that's the same regardless of how far the user dragged
Toast notification from corner saying 'Archived. Undo' (the action already happened — undo is bolted on)
Color change at threshold that pops with a saturation jump
Multiple icons stacked behind the row revealed by progressive swipe distance (overwhelming, hard to read in motion)
Haptic feedback at threshold AND at release AND at completion — overstimulating
ux_reasoning:
The swipe is not a gesture, it's a conversation. The system tells the user 'I see how committed you are' through resistance and visual state, not through binary thresholds. As the user drags, translation maps to drag distance with a curve: f(x) = x for the first 80px, then f(x) = 80 + (x-80) * 0.6 for 80-160px (gentle resistance), then f(x) = 128 + (x-160) * 0.3 beyond (heavy resistance — the row physically resists going further). This is the same model Things 3 uses for its drag-to-complete and it's why their interaction feels alive.

Visual state evolves in three zones, not two: 0-80px shows a faint background tint and the archive icon at 40% opacity, telegraphing the action without committing. 80-160px (the 'will-archive' zone) saturates the bg to full color and brings the icon to full opacity — this is where 95% of intentional swipes release. 160px+ enters 'expanded options' territory where additional actions reveal (snooze, delete) — this is the Mail.app tiered move, but only after you've crossed commitment.

Release behavior is intent-aware. If the user releases between 0-40px, the row snaps back with a stiff spring (200ms, no overshoot — 'never mind'). If released in 40-80px (ambiguous zone), we use a longer spring (300ms) with slight overshoot — the row hesitates before snapping, communicating 'are you sure?' kinetically. Released past 80px: the row continues with momentum into the archive position, then slides up to close the gap (300ms ease-out, no spring on the close — springs in transitions of finality feel wrong).

The undo affordance is not a toast. The archived row remains in place for 1.5s as a thin (8px tall) collapsed strip with the message subject in 11px, and a backwards-pointing chevron. Within those 1.5s, swiping right on the strip or pressing Cmd+Z rolls back. After 1.5s the strip collapses to 0px height over 200ms. This is the 'undo as inline ghost' pattern from Things 3 — it lives where the action happened, not in a corner.

Haptics: single light tap when crossing the 80px commitment threshold. That's it. No haptic at release, none at completion. The translation curve itself IS the haptic.

design_tokens.colors: Zone 1 (0-80px): bg = oklch(0.94 0.03 145 / 0.4), icon = oklch(0.55 0.15 145 / 0.4). Zone 2 (80-160px): bg = oklch(0.75 0.16 145), icon = white at 1.0. Zone 3 (160px+): bg shifts to oklch(0.7 0.18 30) (red) as delete becomes available. Undo strip: oklch(0.96 0.005 250) with oklch(0.5 0.005 250) text.
design_tokens.typography: Undo strip: Inter 11px medium, single line truncated.
design_tokens.spacing: Row height: 56px. Zone thresholds: 80px / 160px. Undo strip height: 8px collapsed, never expanded. Hidden action icon: 24px, anchored 24px from row trailing edge.
design_tokens.motion: Drag: real-time (no animation, just transform applied per pointermove). Snap-back from <40px: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.36, 0, 0.66, 1). Snap from ambiguous zone: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.2, 0.64, 1) with slight overshoot. Commit slide: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1). Undo strip collapse: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1).

component_architecture: <SwipeRow<TItem>> is generic over the item shape. Takes children for the row content + an actions prop ({ archive, snooze, delete }). Uses Pointer Events (not touch + mouse separately) for cross-input parity. Internal state: drag offset (kept in a ref + style mutation, NOT React state — would rerender per pointermove), commitment zone (state, OK to render). <UndoStrip /> mounts when the row commits and unmounts after 1.5s + 200ms collapse, controlled by an external pending-undo queue (so multiple archives can stack their undo windows).
interaction_details: Drag 0-40px: row follows finger 1:1, faint tint appears. Release here: 200ms stiff snap-back, no feedback.
Drag 40-80px: still 1:1 but in 'ambiguous' visual state. Release here: 300ms snap with overshoot — the row visibly hesitates, communicating 'I noticed you started but didn't commit'.
Drag past 80px: single 8ms haptic tap. Resistance kicks in (drag-to-pixel ratio drops to 0.6). Release commits — row slides offscreen at 300ms ease-out.
Drag past 160px: secondary resistance (ratio 0.3) plus bg shifts from green to red — reveals delete option. Most users never reach this; it's there for users who want it.
Post-commit: 8px-tall undo strip appears in-place for 1.5s. Cmd+Z or swipe-right restores. After 1.5s strip collapses over 200ms — total commitment to the action.
accessibility_considerations: role='button' with dynamic aria-label that reflects the current commitment zone — screen readers announce 'in commit zone' as the gesture progresses. Keyboard alternative: when focused, Delete archives directly (no gesture), E for archive, S for snooze. The undo strip uses role='status' aria-live='polite' so it's announced when it appears. Reduced-motion: resistance curve still applies (physics is information, not decoration), but spring overshoot in the ambiguous zone is replaced with a linear ease — no bouncing. Haptic feedback always optional. Color zones are supplemented by icon opacity changes (which are perceivable without color).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Three-zone progressive resistance curve makes the swipe feel like a physical conversation — release behavior actually depends on user intent. 2) The ambiguous-zone snap-back has slight overshoot that communicates 'I noticed you almost did that' without using a tooltip. 3) Undo lives in-place as an 8px strip where the row was, not as a corner toast — the action and its undo share spatial memory.

title: Cohort retention heatmap with interactive cell drill-down and sparkline marginal
design_brief: You're building the cohort retention view for a product analytics tool (think Amplitude + Mixpanel + PostHog). PMs and growth folks live in this view. Pain point: standard heatmaps just gradient-fill cells and call it done — you can see hot/cold but can't actually reason about the cohort. The bar: Amplitude's retention chart (the canonical version), Mode's analytics tiles, Posthog's funnel view.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Rainbow color scale (red-yellow-green) — useless for ordered data and bad for colorblind users
Cell values rendered in 12px white-on-color, unreadable in low-retention cells
Tooltip on hover that just shows 'Cohort A, Week 3: 42%' — what the cell already says
No marginals — heatmaps without row/column summaries are half a chart
Animating cell colors on mount with a stagger — looks impressive once, gets in the way every other time
Click cell to drill down opens a modal with the user list dumped raw
ux_reasoning:
A cohort heatmap has three audiences for the same data: the skimmer (is retention healthy?), the analyst (which cohort is the outlier?), and the operator (which users from this cell churned and why?). The visualization must serve all three without compromising any.

Color: a single-hue luminance scale (not a rainbow), oklch(0.95 0.02 250) → oklch(0.35 0.18 250). Single hue means colorblind users see the same ordering. Luminance encodes magnitude unambiguously. Critically, cell text color flips at L=0.6 so values are always readable — a small thing but the difference between 'usable' and 'unusable'.

Marginals: every row ends with a sparkline showing that cohort's retention trajectory (Week 1 → Week N) at 80px wide / 16px tall. Every column ends with a tiny bar showing weighted average across cohorts. Marginals turn a heatmap from 'look at the colors' into 'look at the shape' — Tufte's small multiples principle applied surgically.

Drill-down is the key interaction. Click a cell: the heatmap doesn't open a modal. Instead, the clicked cell expands inline into a 240px-tall row that shows a list of 12 sample users from that cell, with a 'View all 1,847' link. The other rows compress (each row shrinks proportionally to give the expanded row space) with a 300ms animation. This is the 'master-detail without leaving context' pattern — the user never loses the heatmap orientation.

Hover: the cell value isn't repeated in a tooltip. Instead, a 1px guide line draws across the row and down the column to the marginal sparkline/bar, with the cell's data point on the sparkline highlighted. The tooltip shows comparative info — 'This cell is 12pp higher than the column avg, 4pp lower than this cohort's W2'. Hover gives you ANALYSIS, not the value you already see.

Rejected: a 3D bar chart 'because cohorts have time'. The whole point of a heatmap is dense 2D scanning — adding a depth axis destroys it. Also rejected: animated transitions when filters change. Filter changes are exploratory; the user wants the new state instantly to compare. Crossfade adds latency without information.

design_tokens.colors: Heatmap scale: oklch(0.95 0.02 250) → oklch(0.35 0.18 250), interpolated in OKLCH (perceptually uniform). Text inversion threshold: L=0.6. Guide lines on hover: oklch(0.4 0.005 250 / 0.5) at 1px dashed. Marginal sparkline: oklch(0.55 0.03 250) stroke, no fill.
design_tokens.typography: Cell values: Inter tabular-nums 11px medium. Axis labels: 11px regular. Drill-down user rows: 12px regular for name, 11px regular for joined-date in oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Cell size: 40px wide × 32px tall — wider than tall optimizes for time-axis scanning. Gap between cells: 1px (subtle separation, doesn't fight the gradient). Marginal sparkline column: 96px wide, separated by a 12px gap from the heatmap.
design_tokens.motion: Drill-down row expansion: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Other rows compressing: same easing, same duration, choreographed. Hover guide lines: instant appearance, 80ms opacity fade-out on mouseleave (prevents flicker when moving between cells).

component_architecture: <RetentionHeatmap<TCohort extends Cohort>> generic over cohort metadata. Children: <HeatmapCell />, <RowSparkline />, <ColumnSummary />, <DrilldownPanel users={...} />. Cell expansion state lives in the heatmap root (only one cell can be drilled at a time, so it's just a string | null). Drill data is fetched on click via a getCellUsers prop (so the heatmap doesn't dictate a data layer). Hover state is local to a HeatmapHoverContext to avoid prop drilling 100s of guide updates.
interaction_details: Hover cell: 1px dashed guide lines draw along the row and column to the marginals; the cell's data point on the row sparkline highlights with a 3px filled dot. Tooltip is comparative ('+12pp vs column avg'), not redundant.

Click cell: that cell becomes the drill-anchor; a 240px panel expands BELOW the cell's row showing 12 sample users in a 2-column grid. Click same cell again: collapse. Click different cell: panel reflows to new row with 300ms transition.
Filter change (e.g., 'last 90 days'): heatmap re-renders INSTANTLY with no crossfade. Exploration deserves immediacy, not choreography.
Empty cells (cohort hasn't reached that week yet): rendered as light-gray with em-dash, never as 0%. Mixing 'no data' with '0% retention' is a cardinal data-viz sin.
Long cohort labels truncate at 120px with title attribute fallback — never wrap (would break heatmap row alignment).
accessibility_considerations: role='grid' on root, role='row' on each cohort row, role='gridcell' on each cell with aria-label encoding both cohort and retention value ('First-week cohort, Week 3, 42% retention'). Cells are real <button> elements — keyboard navigable, focus visible. Arrow keys move between cells (Grid pattern from WAI-ARIA APG). Color is supplemented by the actual numeric value in each cell, so colorblind users read directly. Reduced-motion: drill-down panel snaps open instead of animating. Single-hue scale (not red/green) is colorblind-safe. Text color flips at L=0.6 to maintain ≥4.5:1 contrast on every cell.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Single-hue luminance scale + automatic text color flip means every cell is readable, no exceptions — a problem 90% of heatmaps get wrong. 2) Hover tooltips show comparative analysis ('+12pp vs avg'), not the cell value the user can already read. 3) Click-to-drill expands inline with proportional row compression, so the user never loses heatmap orientation.

title: In-product education via deferred contextual hints with one-shot dismissal memory
design_brief: You're building first-time user education for a complex B2B tool (think Linear + Retool + Notion). Pain point: every product ships a 5-step modal tour that no one reads, plus dozens of tooltip popovers that haunt power users forever. The bar: Linear's progressive feature discovery (no modal tours, ever), Notion's empty-state teaching, Things 3's quiet hints.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Blocking modal tour with Next/Skip buttons at first login
Lottie-animated character that says hi at the bottom-right
Persistent dot indicators (red badges) on every menu item that has a 'New!' feature
Tooltips that re-appear after a 90-day cookie expiration
Generic empty state with a sad illustration + 'You don't have any X yet. Create one!' button
Spotlight overlays that dim the entire UI to highlight one button
ux_reasoning:
Onboarding modals are a confession that the product can't teach itself. The solution is not better modals — it's an interface that teaches through use. Three mechanisms in concert:

First: empty states that are functional demos. An empty 'Issues' view doesn't show a sad illustration — it shows a single mock issue rendered exactly like a real issue, with the title 'Try clicking me to see how issues work'. Clicking it opens the issue detail view, also populated with mock data and a 'This is what an issue looks like — create a real one to replace this' inline hint. The empty state IS the tutorial.

Second: deferred contextual hints. When the user performs an action that has a meaningful keyboard shortcut, we wait until the SECOND time they do it via mouse (proving it's a repeated action), then show a small inline hint near where the action happened: 'Tip: ⌘N also creates an issue'. The hint has no dismiss X — it auto-dismisses after 6 seconds and never returns for that shortcut. This is Linear's discipline: hints are a single-use educational asset, not persistent UI.

Third: state-aware empty zones. A new project's Roadmap view doesn't show a tutorial — it shows a horizontal time scale with a faint 'Drag here to add a milestone' label that fades as soon as the user interacts with anything else on the page. Hint visibility is computed from app state (item count, interaction history), not from a 'has user seen this' boolean.

The hint-dismissal memory is a Bloom filter, not a set. We don't track 'user saw hint X' as discrete records — we track a hash of (userId, hintId, version) in a Bloom filter that's small enough to live in localStorage. When the hint definition changes (new shortcut, new copy), the version bumps and the hint re-arms. Tradeoff: occasional false positive (user sees a hint they already dismissed once). That's acceptable; the alternative (an ever-growing list of 'seen' booleans) is worse.

Rejected: feature flags driving 'first-time UX' branches throughout the codebase. Tested it on a previous project — became unmaintainable in 6 months. The current approach keeps onboarding logic at the leaf-component level via a single useDeferredHint hook. Also rejected: time-based hint scheduling ('show on day 3'). Real users don't have linear sessions; teach when the moment is right, not when the calendar says so.

design_tokens.colors: Hint surface: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) on light, oklch(0.22 0.005 250) on dark. Hint border: 1px solid oklch(0.85 0.005 250) — no glow, no shadow, no brand color. Hint text: oklch(0.4 0.005 250) — softer than body text, signals 'meta'. Shortcut kbd: oklch(0.95 0.005 250) bg with oklch(0.3 0.005 250) text.
design_tokens.typography: Hint text: Inter 12px regular. Kbd: 'JetBrains Mono' 11px. Empty state mock content uses the exact same type ramp as real content — disguise is the point.
design_tokens.spacing: Hint padding: 8px 12px. Margin from anchor: 8px. Empty-state mock content sits in the actual list with normal row spacing.
design_tokens.motion: Hint enter: 200ms ease-out opacity + 4px translateY. Hint exit: 150ms ease-in opacity only. No spring. No bounce.

component_architecture: <DeferredHint hintId='cmd-n-new-issue' version={2} trigger={{ type: 'second-mouse-action', action: 'create-issue' }} anchor={anchorRef} placement='bottom-start'>Tip: ⌘N also creates an issue</DeferredHint>. The component subscribes to an InteractionLogContext that tracks mouse vs keyboard usage per action. Internally uses useBloomFilter() to check/record dismissal. The Bloom filter lives in a HintMemoryProvider that wraps the app. Empty states use <FunctionalEmpty kind='issues'> which knows to render a mock interactive item for the relevant entity type.
interaction_details: First time the user clicks 'New Issue': nothing happens (no hint). The system is observing, not preaching.
Second time clicking 'New Issue': 500ms later, the hint appears below the button: 'Tip: ⌘N also creates an issue'. 6 seconds later it fades out and never returns.
If the user uses ⌘N before triggering the second mouse-click, the hint is silently dismissed — they already discovered it.

Empty issue list: shows ONE mock issue with title 'Try clicking me — this is what an issue looks like'. Clicking opens the detail view in 'preview mode' with a single 'Create your first real issue' affordance.
Hint copy version bumps: when the team rewords a hint or adds a new shortcut, version increments and previously-dismissed users see it once more. This is intentional.
accessibility_considerations: Hint uses role='status' aria-live='polite' so screen readers announce it without interrupting. Hint is keyboard-dismissible via Esc when focus is anywhere on the page. The empty-state mock issue is a real <button> with descriptive aria-label ('Example issue. Click to see how the detail view works'). Reduced-motion: hint appears instantly, no translateY. The 'second mouse action' threshold is replaced with 'first mouse action' for keyboard-primary users (detected via initial input mode) — keyboard users get hints faster because they're more likely to benefit. Contrast: hint text at 4.5:1, kbd inside hint at 7:1.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Hints fire on behavior signals ('used mouse twice for an action with a shortcut'), not on time or first-login. 2) Bloom filter for dismissal memory keeps localStorage tiny and gracefully degrades — false positives are acceptable, false negatives (re-showing dismissed hints) are not. 3) Empty states are functional mocks, not illustrations — the empty state IS the tutorial.

title: Multi-step business onboarding with branch-aware progress and inline server-error recovery
design_brief: You're building the merchant onboarding flow for a payments platform (think Stripe Connect onboarding, Vercel project setup, Plaid Link). 27 fields across 6 steps, but branches: a sole proprietor sees 11 fields, an LLC sees 18, a regulated entity sees 27. Pain point: every multi-step form ships with a top progress bar showing '3 of 7' that lies the moment the form branches. The bar: Stripe Connect (the canonical implementation), Plaid Link's identity flow, Mercury's account opening.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Numeric progress bar (Step 3 of 7) that doesn't account for branching
'Save and continue' button at the bottom of each step — engineers don't have a save button in their life
Validation errors that scroll-jump to the first error on submit
Generic 'Something went wrong' for server errors with no recovery path
One-page-per-step with hard navigation — user can't see context from the previous step
Required asterisks on every field with no positive affordance for completed fields
Disabled submit button with no explanation until you hover it
ux_reasoning:
The hardest problem in long forms isn't visual — it's the user's mental model of completion. A linear progress bar lies about branching forms; users abandon when 'step 3 of 7' suddenly becomes 'step 3 of 12' because they ticked an LLC checkbox.

Solution: a TIME-based progress indicator, not a step count. We show 'About 4 minutes left' computed from the remaining required fields × the user's per-field median completion time (collected from their session). On branch expansion (user selects a more complex entity type), the time estimate updates smoothly over 400ms — communicating the change honestly without alarm. This is the Stripe Connect move and it's why their abandonment rates are industry-leading.

Step navigation is non-linear. The left sidebar shows all step titles with state icons: completed (filled check), in-progress (open ring), upcoming (faint number), blocked (lock with 'unlocks after step 2'). The user can jump to any unblocked step. Branched steps appear with a subtle indent under their parent. This is borrowed from Pitch's onboarding and Linear's settings nav.

Validation philosophy: validate on blur, never on keystroke. Show success state (faint green check inside the input, no banner) the moment a field becomes valid. Error state appears on blur, but ONLY if the user previously had focus AND moved away — never on initial render, never as 'this field is required' before the user touched it. Errors live inline below the field in 12px text, never as a tooltip.

Server-side error recovery is the hardest case. When you submit and the server says 'EIN format invalid' for a field you thought was fine, naive forms show a banner at the top and scroll there. Better: we mark the field with error state, scroll it into view with 64px of context above (so the user sees what step they're on), focus the input, and show an actionable error message — 'EIN should be 9 digits without dashes. Try removing the dashes.' with an inline 'Fix automatically' button if the fix is deterministic.

Array fields (e.g., 'Add a beneficial owner') use the 'add an empty card that focuses immediately' pattern, not a modal. Each card has a minimize affordance so a user with 8 owners can collapse the ones they've finished. Drag-to-reorder uses pointer events with a 200ms delay so accidental clicks don't become drags.

Rejected: a single long scrollable form. Tested — abandonment skyrockets past 12 visible fields. Also rejected: Formik / React Hook Form generic patterns. They couple too tightly to validation. Our state lives in a typed reducer per step so branching logic is local and testable.

design_tokens.colors: Input border: oklch(0.85 0.005 250) idle, oklch(0.5 0.005 250) focus, oklch(0.6 0.18 25) error, oklch(0.6 0.14 145) valid. Step nav: completed icon oklch(0.55 0.14 145), in-progress oklch(0.55 0.005 250), upcoming oklch(0.75 0.005 250). Critical: NO red bg fills on errors — only border + inline text.
design_tokens.typography: Field labels: Inter 13px medium. Inputs: 14px regular. Error/help text: 12px regular. Step nav titles: 13px medium. Time-remaining indicator: 11px regular tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Input height: 36px. Vertical gap between fields: 16px. Field group (related fields like address): 8px between fields, 24px before next group. Step content max-width: 560px (Stripe's exact number).
design_tokens.motion: Field validation state change: 150ms ease-out on border-color and icon opacity. Step transition: 250ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) horizontal slide. Time estimate update: 400ms ease-out tween on the digits using FLIP. Server error scroll: 'smooth' behavior with custom 320ms duration.

component_architecture: <MultiStepForm<TSchema extends FieldSchema>> generic over the full schema. <StepNav steps={branchedSteps} /> reads from a FormStateContext that holds the typed state per step. Each <Step> is its own component that subscribes to the slice it owns via a useFormSlice<TKey>() hook. <Field<TValue>> is generic with a validator prop. Branching is declared on the schema itself: { id: 'entity_type', revealsSteps: (val) => val === 'llc' ? ['llc_details', 'beneficial_owners'] : [] }. Time estimation lives in a useTimeRemaining() hook that reads field count + a moving-average per-field time.
interaction_details: First-time field focus: starts a timer. On blur, the elapsed time feeds the running median used by the time-remaining indicator.
Field becomes valid: border shifts to muted green over 150ms, a small check appears in the input. No banner, no celebration.

Submit with server error: target field's border turns red, page scrolls to put the field 64px below the viewport top (showing step context), and focus moves to the input. Error text appears below with an actionable 'Fix automatically' button if the error is mechanical (e.g., dashes in EIN).
Branching trigger (selecting LLC): sidebar reveals new steps with a subtle 200ms slide-in animation; time-remaining number tweens upward over 400ms with a tiny ↑ icon next to it for 1.5s.
Save state: every input change is debounced 800ms and PATCHed to the server. The 'About 4 minutes left' indicator briefly shows 'Saved' in oklch(0.55 0.14 145) for 1s on each successful save.
accessibility_considerations: Each input has a proper <label htmlFor>, aria-invalid bound to error state, aria-describedby pointing to the inline error. Errors use role='alert' so screen readers announce them when they appear. Step nav is a <nav aria-label='Onboarding steps'> with each step a real link; the current step has aria-current='step'. Reduced-motion: step transitions become instant, time-remaining tweens become snap updates. Color is supplemented: error icon is unique, valid check icon is unique, step states have distinct icons (not just color). Focus order follows visual order; arrow keys move between fields when focus is on a select.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Time-remaining indicator computed from the user's own per-field completion median — honest about branching changes instead of lying with step counts. 2) Server errors include an inline 'Fix automatically' button for deterministic fixes (dashes in EINs, whitespace, etc.) — meeting the user where the problem actually is. 3) Validation only fires on blur after first focus; success state is silent (no banner, just a faint check) — the form respects the user's attention.

title: 3D product configurator with section-snapped scroll and material swatch live-preview
design_brief: You're building the product configurator for a furniture e-commerce site (think Herman Miller's chair builder, Apple's product gallery, Tesla's configurator). Users need to feel the materiality before buying. Pain point: most 3D configurators ship a hero canvas + a sidebar of options that updates the model — fine for spec sheets, wrong for the emotional decision of 'is this the right chair for my house?'. The bar: Apple's product detail pages, Herman Miller's Sayl/Embody configurators, the Aviator chair landing page by Linear's team.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Static 3D viewer with orbit controls dropped on the page like a YouTube embed
Loading state showing a skeleton box for 6 seconds while the GLB downloads
Color swatches that change the model instantly with a sharp transition — destroys the material illusion
Hero spin-on-load that no user asked for
Generic 'Add to cart' floating button that ignores the spatial composition
HDR environment maps with so much intensity the product looks like a CGI render, not furniture
ux_reasoning:
The configurator is a STORY about a product, told spatially. Scroll is the narrative engine: each scroll section advances both the camera position around the chair AND a textual annotation ('the seat tilt mechanism', 'the lumbar support', 'the materials'). The camera path is hand-authored as a Bezier curve with keyframed positions per section — this is the 'product as protagonist' technique Apple uses on iPhone pages.

Three.js setup discipline: GLTF model loaded with DRACO compression + KTX2 textures, total <2MB. Suspense boundary shows a low-res preview JPG from a hand-picked angle (not a spinner, not a skeleton). The transition from preview to 3D is a crossfade at 600ms — slow enough to feel like emergence, fast enough to not annoy. Camera always uses an orthographic component during transitions so the product doesn't 'breathe' from perspective changes.

Material swatches are the hardest UX. Clicking a swatch shouldn't instantly swap the material — that breaks the illusion. Instead, we animate the swap as a directional wipe (TextureTransitionShader) over 400ms, sweeping the new material across the surface as if revealed by light. This is borrowed from Karim Maaloul's case studies. The swatch UI itself uses tiny 32x32 rendered samples of the actual material on the actual mesh topology (not flat color squares) — Apple's move.

Interaction model: scroll drives the camera, but the user can hijack with drag. While dragging, the camera path 'detaches' and the user controls orbit freely. When they release, the camera lerps back to the nearest section anchor over 700ms with ease-out — they never get lost in space. This is the killer move: freedom on demand, narrative on release.

Performance: render only on demand (frameloop='demand'), invalidate on scroll, drag, and material transitions. We hit 60fps on integrated GPUs because we're not rendering during idle. Pixel ratio capped at min(devicePixelRatio, 2).

Rejected: a full-page WebGL takeover with the page chrome inside the canvas. Tested it — scroll feels wrong (can't trust the browser's scrollbar), accessibility nightmare, SEO disaster. The canvas is a CHARACTER on the page, not the stage. Also rejected: AR mode by default. Most users are on desktop; AR is a feature flag for mobile.

design_tokens.colors: Canvas environment lighting: oklch(0.96 0.005 80) main, oklch(0.4 0.02 240) fill, soft ambient at 0.4 intensity. Page bg: oklch(0.98 0.003 80) — slightly warm white so the product reads as 'in a real room'. NEVER pure white #fff (kills depth). Material swatch ring on hover: oklch(0.4 0.005 80) at 1px.
design_tokens.typography: Section annotations: serif (e.g., 'Tiempos', 'Newsreader') 22px / 32px — editorial register. Material names: Inter 12px uppercase tracking-wider. Price ticker: Inter 14px tabular-nums.
design_tokens.spacing: Canvas occupies viewport-left 60%, annotations occupy right 40%. Sections are 100vh each. Swatch size: 40px circle with 4px gap.
design_tokens.motion: Scroll-driven camera: lerp factor 0.08 per frame (smooth follow, not instant). Drag release snap-back: 700ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Material transition wipe: 400ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1). Crossfade preview→3D: 600ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <Configurator product={...}> root sets up a single <Canvas frameloop='demand' />. Children: <CameraRig path={bezierPath} sectionAnchors={[]} />, <Product mesh={gltf} material={current} transitionTo={pending} />, <Annotations sections={[]} />, <SwatchPanel materials={[]} onSelect={...} />. <CameraRig /> reads scroll progress from a ScrollContext and uses useFrame to interpolate camera position along the Bezier. Drag detaches and reattaches via a useGesture binding that flips a 'manual control' atom. Materials are pre-loaded via useLoader + Suspense.
interaction_details: Page load: low-res JPG preview shows instantly. 3D canvas fades in over 600ms once first frame is rendered. No spinner ever shows.
Scroll: camera smoothly traverses the authored Bezier path. Scroll speed maps to path traversal speed with a 0.08 lerp — smooth even on touchpad inertia.
Drag on canvas: camera path detaches; user gets full orbit control. Release: 700ms ease-out lerp back to the nearest section anchor.
Material swatch click: shader-driven directional wipe sweeps the new material across the chair over 400ms. The swatch itself is a tiny rendered sample of the actual material — not a flat color.
Hover over annotation text: the corresponding part of the model receives a subtle 1.05x emissive highlight for 200ms — text and 3D are bound.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label describing the product. All material swatches are <button> with aria-label='Walnut, leather. Currently selected.' or similar. Section annotations are real <section> elements with semantic <h2>, so screen reader users get the same narrative via headings + bodies — they don't need the 3D. Reduced-motion: scroll-driven camera locks to each anchor (snap behavior), material transitions become instant, no shader animation. Keyboard navigation moves between swatches with arrow keys; Enter selects. The configurator also exposes a high-contrast 'specs only' tab that's a pure text product summary.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Material swatches are tiny rendered previews of the actual material on the actual mesh — not flat color squares, so they communicate the real look. 2) Directional wipe shader for material transitions feels like the chair is being draped in new fabric, not jump-swapped. 3) Drag detaches the camera from the scroll narrative, release smoothly snaps to the nearest anchor — freedom on demand, story on release.

title: Issue list with cursor-driven keyboard nav on desktop and gesture-driven peek on mobile
design_brief: You're building the issue list view for a developer project tracker (think Linear + GitHub Issues + Height). The same component must feel native on a 27" 4K display with a mouse + keyboard AND on a 6" phone with one thumb. Pain point: most 'responsive' apps just shrink desktop layouts to a column and call it mobile. The bar: Linear's iOS app (whose interaction model is completely different from web yet feels like the same product), Things 3 across iOS/macOS, Notion's mobile vs desktop.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same component with breakpoint media queries flipping flex direction
Mobile menu = hamburger that opens a drawer with the same desktop nav inside
Long-press on mobile to access desktop's right-click context menu — undiscoverable
Mobile bottom bar with 5 icons + labels copying desktop sidebar nav
Modal sheets that take 80% of the screen for a single action
Mobile filters that open a full-screen page with the same form layout as desktop
ux_reasoning:
Same data, two interaction philosophies. On desktop, the user has a precise pointer and 100+ keystrokes per minute — the UI should reward density and keyboard mastery. On mobile, the user has a thumb and partial attention — the UI should reward gesture and reduce decisions. Pretending these are the same problem is the original sin of 'responsive design'.

Desktop model: rows are 32px tall, all-keyboard navigable. j/k moves selection. Enter opens the issue. Cmd+Enter opens it in a side panel without leaving the list. Right-click is real (custom context menu). Hover shows quick actions (assign, priority, status) on the right side of the row. List supports multi-select with Shift+click and bulk actions via a Cmd+K command palette scoped to selection.

Mobile model: rows are 64px tall with two lines (title + meta). The 'quick actions' aren't visible — they're revealed by a horizontal swipe (Apple Mail pattern: left-swipe reveals priority + assign, right-swipe reveals status). Tap opens the issue full-screen with a slide transition. Long-press starts multi-select mode (haptic confirms entry). The bottom of the screen is a 'peek toolbar' that's always-visible at 40px tall with three context actions — never a separate page or drawer.

The trick: the COMPONENT is the same; the INTERACTION ADAPTER changes. We use a useInputModality hook that returns 'touch' | 'mouse' based on the last pointer event type (not screen width — a touch laptop should get touch behavior). The IssueRow component takes its interaction adapter as a prop and renders different affordances accordingly. Layout dimensions are media-query driven (different problem from interaction modality), but interaction is modality-driven.

Filter sheet on mobile is the killer detail. Instead of a full-screen page or a bottom drawer, we use a 'half sheet' that occupies bottom 50% and lets the user see the filtered list updating live as they tap chips. This is the Things 3 + Apple Mail pattern. Filters apply optimistically; the sheet stays open. To dismiss, drag down — never a 'Done' button (it would compete with the live preview).

Rejected: a unified gesture system where 'long-press' means 'context menu' across mobile and desktop. Desktop users have a right-click; forcing them to learn long-press is hostile. Modality-specific affordances respect each input. Also rejected: shipping a 'Lite' mobile version with fewer features. Mobile users don't want less; they want different.

design_tokens.colors: Shared: bg-surface-0, fg-body, status-* hues. Mobile-specific: a 'sheet handle' color oklch(0.85 0.005 250) (the small horizontal bar at top of bottom sheets). Desktop hover bg: oklch(0.96 0.005 250). Mobile swipe-action bg: oklch(0.6 0.18 145) for primary, oklch(0.6 0.16 60) for secondary.
design_tokens.typography: Desktop rows: Inter 13px. Mobile rows: Inter 15px title + 12px meta. Touch-friendly type scale on mobile (15px minimum to avoid iOS zoom-on-focus).
design_tokens.spacing: Desktop row: 32px height, 12px horizontal padding, 4px between rows. Mobile row: 64px height, 16px padding, 8px between rows. Mobile touch target minimum: 44px.
design_tokens.motion: Desktop: 80ms hover transitions, no spring. Mobile: swipe gestures use 1:1 finger tracking, snap-back uses 200ms ease-out, full-screen push uses iOS-style 350ms cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1).

component_architecture: <IssueRow<TItem>> generic. Takes an InteractionAdapter as a prop. Two adapter implementations: DesktopAdapter (renders hover quick-actions, listens for keyboard nav) and TouchAdapter (renders nothing on the row, handles swipe gestures via Pointer Events). useInputModality() hook returns 'touch' | 'mouse'; <IssueList /> reads it once and picks the adapter. Layout dimensions come from Tailwind responsive classes (sm: / md: prefixes). Filter sheet is <FilterSheet half={true|false} /> — half on mobile, side-panel on desktop.
interaction_details: Desktop: j/k navigate, Enter opens, Cmd+Enter opens in side panel, hover reveals action icons on row right, right-click shows custom context menu.
Mobile: tap opens full-screen, left-swipe reveals priority+assign, right-swipe reveals status, long-press enters multi-select with haptic confirmation.
Modality switch mid-session (user plugs in a mouse on iPad): next pointer event updates the adapter — row affordances change live, no reload needed.
Filter sheet on mobile: half-height bottom sheet with live preview of filtered results visible above. Drag down to dismiss (no Done button).
Filter on desktop: side panel slides in from the right, list reflows. Both can apply filters optimistically and undo via Cmd+Z.
accessibility_considerations: Role='listbox' with role='option' rows. aria-selected reflects keyboard selection. The same component announces correctly to screen readers in both modalities. Touch swipe actions have equivalent keyboard shortcuts on the linked desktop adapter (e for archive, a for assign). VoiceOver/TalkBack on mobile: swipe actions are exposed via the rotor as custom actions — not just gesture-only. Reduced-motion: mobile transition becomes a snap, no slide. Focus order: matches DOM order (top to bottom). Touch targets minimum 44px on mobile, with full row hit-area.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Modality (touch vs mouse) is detected from the last pointer event, not from screen width — a touch laptop gets touch behavior live without reload. 2) The component is shared; only the InteractionAdapter changes — same data model, two interaction philosophies, no code duplication. 3) Filter sheet on mobile is half-height with live preview above, instead of a modal page — the user can see results while filtering.

title: Screen-reader-first data table with live filter narration and reduced-motion alternate flow
design_brief: You're building the customer-list table for a CRM (think Attio + Salesforce + Hubspot). 40% of users at the largest accounts use screen readers (a real number from enterprise procurement requirements). Pain point: most tables ship as `<div role='table'>` with ARIA bolted on as an afterthought — keyboard nav is broken, filter changes are silent, and reduced-motion fallbacks are 'animation: none'. The bar: GitHub's accessibility work (the gold standard for screen-reader UX in dev tools), Apple's macOS HIG, the Adobe Spectrum library.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using div+role='cell' when a real <td> would work — semantic HTML is faster than ARIA
aria-live='assertive' on filter changes (rude — interrupts whatever the user was reading)
Focus traps that don't release on Esc
Skip-link that's visually hidden but never reachable by keyboard tab order
Reduced-motion = no animation at all (often makes UI more confusing, not less)
Color-coded statuses without text alternatives because 'we use aria-label on the color dot'
ux_reasoning:
Accessibility isn't a checklist — it's an interaction model parallel to the visual one. A great table for a screen reader user is structurally identical to a great table for a sighted user, with the additional property that the SR user can hear the structure and changes.

First: semantic HTML over ARIA. Real <table><thead><tbody> with proper <th scope='col'> and <th scope='row'>. Screen readers ALREADY know how to navigate tables (Ctrl+Alt+arrow in JAWS, VO+arrow in VoiceOver). When you replace this with divs+ARIA, you break those interactions. Hard rule on this project: ARIA only when no semantic element exists.

Live filter narration: when the user applies a filter, we update an aria-live='polite' region with a short, useful message: 'Filtered to 142 of 1,827 customers by status: Active.' Critical word: 'polite' — assertive would interrupt the user mid-reading. The narration is debounced 600ms so rapid filter changes (typing in search) coalesce into a single 'Filtered to 23 customers' announcement at the end.

Keyboard navigation: the table follows the WAI-ARIA Grid pattern. Tab enters/exits the grid (one tab stop for the whole table — critical for users who don't want to tab through 1000 cells). Arrow keys move between cells. Enter activates the focused cell's primary action. F2 enters edit mode for editable cells. Esc exits edit mode. Home/End move to row start/end; Ctrl+Home/End to grid corners.

Reduced-motion is not 'no animation' — it's an alternate flow. Default state-change animations (sort, filter, row expand) are choreographed for clarity: rows fade as they reflow. With reduced-motion, we DON'T just kill the animation — we add an instant 'highlight pulse' on the affected rows for 600ms (background opacity flash) so the user can still tell what changed. Removing motion without replacement removes information.

Focus management on async operations: when the user triggers a server action (delete a row), focus must land somewhere sensible. We move it to the next row, or the previous row if there is no next, or to the 'Add customer' button if the list is empty. This is the kind of detail VoiceOver users notice instantly.

Rejected: a 'high contrast mode' that swaps the palette. Modern OSes (Windows High Contrast, macOS Increase Contrast) handle this at the system level; an app-level toggle is a duplicate that often fights with the user's system setting. We use forced-colors media query to ensure the table works in HC mode, and that's it.

design_tokens.colors: Standard table palette + forced-colors fallbacks. Focus ring: 2px outline at 2px offset, oklch(0.55 0.18 250) — meets 3:1 contrast against all surface variants. NEVER use box-shadow for focus rings (forced-colors strips them).
design_tokens.typography: Body: Inter 13px / 20px line-height. Table headers: 11px uppercase tracking-wider semi-bold. Never below 12px for any interactive text.
design_tokens.spacing: Row: 36px height (touch-friendly even on desktop). Cell padding: 12px horizontal, 4px vertical. Sortable header has 24px target zone via padding even though the visible arrow icon is 12px.
design_tokens.motion: Default: 200ms row reflow, 150ms filter pill enter. Reduced-motion: instant reflow + 600ms background highlight pulse on changed rows.

component_architecture: <DataTable<TRow extends { id: string }> columns={[]} rows={[]} />. Columns declare { key, header, accessor, sortable, editable, format }. Internal state: focused cell coords (row, col), filter narration message. The narration is broadcast via a useNarration() hook that wraps a debounced setter on a single aria-live container. <Cell /> is generic with a custom renderer prop. Sort/filter logic is pure (no animation library), and the animation is purely opt-in via a useMotionPreference() hook.
interaction_details: Tab into the table: focus lands on the first cell (or last-focused cell on return). One tab stop for the whole table.
Arrow keys move between cells; Home/End to row edges; Ctrl+Home/End to grid corners. Delete on a row triggers onDelete and narrates the action.
Filter typing: narration is debounced 600ms so the screen reader announces 'Filtered to 23 rows' once at the end, not on every keystroke.
Sort header click: announces 'Sorted by Status'. aria-sort updates so screen readers know the current sort state.
Reduced-motion users: row reflow happens instantly, then a 600ms background pulse highlights the rows that changed — keeping the 'what changed' signal without the kinetic animation.
accessibility_considerations: Semantic <table><thead><tbody><th scope='col'> structure — every screen reader knows this. role='grid' adds keyboard nav semantics. aria-sort tracks current sort. aria-rowcount + aria-rowindex for virtualized scenarios. Focus ring is a 2px outline (not box-shadow) so it survives forced-colors mode. Reduced-motion swaps animations for an instant transition + 600ms background pulse so the change is still perceivable. Live region debounced so it's never noisy. WCAG AAA contrast on body text and focus indicators.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Real <table> semantics, not div+role — screen reader users get native table nav for free. 2) Filter narration is debounced 600ms so rapid typing produces one announcement, not 12. 3) Reduced-motion is an ALTERNATE flow (instant reflow + 600ms highlight pulse), not motion removed — information preserved without kinetics.

title: Optimistic comment submission with rollback choreography and conflict resolution
design_brief: You're building the comment thread for a code review tool (think Linear comments + GitHub PR review + Graphite). Pain point: most comment UIs either show a spinner during submit (sluggish), or 'optimistically' insert the comment but provide zero recovery if the server rejects it. The bar: Linear (comments appear instantly, undo works, server errors are visible without being modal), Things 3's offline-first sync, Apple Notes' multi-device convergence.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Submit button with a spinner inside it that locks the UI for 800ms
Toast 'Comment posted' after server confirmation — meaningless when the comment is already visible
Hard reload on conflict — wipes out user's draft
Generic 'Failed to post' error that disappears in 4 seconds
Optimistic UI with no visual difference between 'sent' and 'confirmed' states — the user can't tell what's real
Disabled submit until 'something is typed' — the system already knows from the empty state
ux_reasoning:
Optimistic UI is not 'pretend the server said yes'. It's a contract: 'I'm going to assume this works, and if it doesn't, I'll choreograph the rollback in a way that respects your draft and your attention'. Getting the contract right is the difference between feeling fast and feeling unstable.

Three states per comment: pending (just submitted, awaiting server), confirmed (server acked, same as any other comment), failed (server rejected, needs attention). Visual encoding: pending has a 60% opacity author name + a 1px shimmer along the left edge (subtle — pending shouldn't scream); confirmed has full opacity; failed has full opacity but with a small red dot next to the timestamp and an inline action row 'Retry · Edit · Discard'.

The critical decision: pending comments are FULL participants in the thread. Other users see them too (over websocket) with the same pending state. Replies to a pending comment are themselves pending and form a chain. This is essential for collaborative speed — Figma, Linear, and Graphite all do this. The alternative (queuing local pending comments and inserting on ack) introduces a jarring layout shift when the comment appears.

Conflict resolution: when the server rejects (e.g., the thread was locked between draft and submit), we don't dump the user back to a blank composer. We surface the failed comment in place with its full text intact, mark it failed, and add an inline 'Discard or Edit'. The user's words are never lost — that's the cardinal sin of optimistic UI done wrong.

Draft persistence: as the user types, the draft is written to a per-thread localStorage key, debounced 400ms. If the tab crashes, returning users see their draft restored with a faint 'Restored from draft 2m ago' line above the composer. Cleared on successful submit.

Motion: comments slide in with a 200ms ease-out + 4px translateY. Pending shimmer is a slow (2s) low-amplitude opacity wave on the left edge — visible peripherally but not distracting. Server confirm: shimmer stops over 100ms, opacity ramps to full over 200ms — the comment 'settles'. Failure: comment shakes 8px horizontal over 300ms (the only place we use a shake), then the red dot appears.

Rejected: a queued-actions sidebar listing pending requests. Tested it — users don't want a dashboard of their pending operations; they want to see the operation in context. Also rejected: silent retry. We retry once silently (network blip recovery), but a second failure surfaces to the user. Magic that hides errors is a debt that comes due during real incidents.

design_tokens.colors: Pending shimmer: oklch(0.85 0.005 250) traveling gradient. Confirmed: same as any comment. Failed indicator: 8px dot oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Failed action row: oklch(0.55 0.005 250) text with 'Discard' in oklch(0.55 0.16 25).
design_tokens.typography: Comment body: Inter 14px / 22px. Author: 13px medium. Timestamp: 12px regular oklch(0.55). Failed inline actions: 12px medium.
design_tokens.spacing: Comment block: 12px vertical padding, 16px horizontal. Composer height: 80px minimum, grows. Shimmer band width: 3px on left edge.
design_tokens.motion: Comment enter: 200ms cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1) opacity + 4px translateY. Pending shimmer: 2000ms linear infinite. Confirm settle: 100ms shimmer stop + 200ms opacity ramp. Failure shake: 300ms cubic-bezier(0.36, 0.07, 0.19, 0.97) translateX keyframes [0, -4, 4, -3, 3, -2, 2, 0].

component_architecture: <CommentThread<TComment extends BaseComment>> generic. Each comment carries a status field: 'pending' | 'confirmed' | 'failed'. <Comment /> renders different affordances per status. Submission is via a useOptimisticSubmit() hook that immediately appends a pending comment to the local list, fires the server request, and updates status on response. Draft persistence is via useThreadDraft(threadId). Conflict UI lives inline on the failed comment, not as a toast.
interaction_details: Submit: comment appears instantly with pending visual state (60% opacity author, 3px shimmer on left). Composer clears.
Server confirms: shimmer stops smoothly, opacity ramps to full over 200ms — the comment 'settles' into the thread.
Server fails (after 1 silent retry): comment shakes 300ms, red dot appears next to timestamp, inline 'Retry · Discard' row reveals. Body text is preserved exactly.
Tab crash recovery: returning to the thread shows the composer with the draft restored and a faint 'Restored from draft 2m ago' line above it.
Other users see pending comments too (with same shimmer) over websocket. Replies to pending comments are also pending and chain correctly.
accessibility_considerations: Pending comments use aria-busy='true' so screen readers know the content state. Failed comments include role='alert' with the actionable retry/discard. Live region announces 'Comment posted' on confirm but only when focus has moved away from the composer (so it doesn't interrupt typing). Keyboard: Cmd+Enter submits from anywhere in the composer; Esc with a pending failed comment focused fires Discard. Reduced-motion: comment enter, shimmer, and shake animations all disabled — visual state still encoded via opacity + dot, not just motion. Color is supplemented: failure indicator is a dot AND a text label.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Pending comments are full thread participants visible to other users — no jarring 'insert on confirm' layout shift. 2) Failure preserves the user's text and surfaces inline retry/discard at the comment, not as a corner toast — the failure lives where the action lived. 3) Single silent retry handles network blips invisibly, but second failure surfaces — magic doesn't hide real errors.

title: Editorial long-read article layout with marginal annotations and chromatic typesetting
design_brief: You're building the article page for a long-form publication (think Pudding.cool, Quanta Magazine, Aeon, NYT Magazine digital). Editors and readers expect a layout that reads like a designed book, not a Medium post. Pain point: 99% of CMS templates ship a single column of body text with a sticky share bar and call it 'editorial'. The bar: Pudding.cool's data-driven stories, Quanta's mathematical typography, FiveThirtyEight's annotated visualizations, Robin Sloan's newsletter design.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single column of 16px body text spanning full content width
Lucide social-share icons in a sticky left rail
Subscribe modal triggered on 30s timer
Generic pull-quotes in italic with vertical bars on the side
Footnote popovers that obscure the text they belong to
'Estimated 7 min read' label that destroys the editorial register
Lazy-loaded images that pop in with a 200ms blur transition
ux_reasoning:
Editorial design is about RHYTHM — the alternation between dense type, breathing room, marginal voices, and inline media. Most digital long-reads are a wall of text because they treat the page as an article container rather than a designed spread.

Layout: a 12-column grid where body text occupies columns 4-9 (6 columns wide, ~620px), and columns 1-3 are reserved for marginalia (annotations, definitions, author asides). The marginalia column is OPTIONAL per paragraph — a paragraph without an annotation just leaves that margin empty, which is fine. Columns 10-12 are for floats: pull-quotes, inline charts, sidebar definitions. This is the book layout transposed to web; Pudding.cool does this beautifully.

Typography: serif body (Tiempos Text, Newsreader, or Source Serif) at 18px / 30px line-height. Sans-serif for marginalia (Söhne or Inter) at 13px / 18px — establishing two distinct voices via type alone. Numerals are old-style figures in body text (more elegant) and tabular lining figures in any data display (more legible). Drop cap on the opening paragraph: 4-line tall, custom font, hand-positioned with first-letter::first-line tweaking.

Chromatic typesetting: terms with definitions are typeset in a slightly warmer ink (oklch(0.25 0.04 30) vs body's oklch(0.18 0.005 30)) — the difference is barely perceptible but the eye picks up the rhythm. Hover OR tap on a colored term reveals its definition in the right margin (column 10) with a 1px hairline connecting term to definition. This is the FiveThirtyEight inline-definition pattern done right.

Footnotes don't pop over. They float in the right margin aligned to the line that references them, just like a printed book. On mobile (where there's no margin), footnotes collapse into expandable inline spans that toggle below the paragraph on tap.

Scroll behavior: NO scroll-driven animations. NO parallax. NO fade-in-on-scroll for paragraphs. Editorial work is read; it should not perform. The single exception: the article's opening photo has a subtle 'Ken Burns' (1.05x zoom over 30s) only while it's in the viewport, evoking documentary film. This is one moment of motion, used surgically.

Images have hand-typeset captions (italic, 13px, indented from image left) — never centered, never with copyright on a separate line.

Rejected: a dark-mode toggle. Long reads in dark mode are factually worse for sustained reading (most type research backs this). We respect prefers-color-scheme but our dark mode uses warm off-white text on warm dark background (not pure white on black), and the typography ramp shifts slightly heavier in dark to maintain perceptual weight.

design_tokens.colors: Body ink: oklch(0.18 0.005 30) light / oklch(0.88 0.005 60) dark (warm off-white). Page bg: oklch(0.985 0.003 80) light (warm paper white) / oklch(0.18 0.005 80) dark. Definition terms: oklch(0.25 0.04 30) — a 'warmer ink'. Marginalia: oklch(0.45 0.005 250) — cooler, recedes. Hairlines: 1px oklch(0.85 0.005 250).
design_tokens.typography: Body: 'Tiempos Text', 'Newsreader', Georgia, serif. 18px / 30px / -0.005em. Marginalia: 'Söhne', Inter, sans-serif. 13px / 18px. Drop cap: 'Tiempos Headline' 96px, 4-line height. Old-style figures (font-variant-numeric: oldstyle-nums) in body.
design_tokens.spacing: 12-column grid, 24px gutters, max-width 1280px centered. Body column: cols 4-9. Marginalia: cols 1-3. Floats: cols 10-12. Paragraph spacing: 0 (no margin-bottom; new paragraphs are signaled by first-line indent — the book convention).
design_tokens.motion: Single intentional animation: opening hero image Ken Burns 1.05x scale over 30000ms ease-out, only while in viewport. Nothing else moves. No scroll triggers, no parallax.

component_architecture: <Article /> root sets up the 12-column grid. <Paragraph /> accepts optional annotation and footnote children that render in margins via CSS Grid positioning. <Term term='entropy' definition='...' /> is an inline span that registers with a DefinitionContext so the matching <DefinitionMargin /> can render at the right position. <Footnote n={1} /> + <FootnoteContent n={1}>...</FootnoteContent> are similarly paired. Mobile layout collapses margins into expandable inline disclosures.
interaction_details: Read flow: pure typographic. No motion triggered by scrolling. No reading-progress bar.
Hero image: 30s Ken Burns zoom (1.0 → 1.05x), only while in viewport, runs once. The single intentional animation.
Term hover/tap: definition appears in right margin on desktop with a 1px hairline drawn from term to definition. On mobile, definition expands inline below the paragraph as a disclosure.
Footnote: rendered in right margin aligned to its paragraph on desktop. On mobile, tappable superscript that expands a sentence-style note below the paragraph.
Print stylesheet: the layout already IS a print layout. @media print just removes interactive affordances (term underlines, footnote buttons become plain text).
accessibility_considerations: Real semantic HTML: <article>, <aside> for marginalia and footnotes, <figure> + <figcaption> for images, <sup> for footnote markers. Terms are <button aria-expanded> with role='definition' on the popover. Reading order in DOM matches narrative order (paragraph, then its annotation, then its footnote) so screen readers traverse naturally. Reduced-motion: Ken Burns disabled. Color contrast: body ink at 14:1 on paper (AAA). Definition terms at 7:1 (AAA) — the warmer ink is still high contrast. Mobile expansions are real disclosure widgets with proper ARIA. Type minimum: 18px body (well above 16px floor), 13px marginalia.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) 12-column grid with optional left-margin annotations and right-margin footnotes — actual editorial layout, not single-column. 2) Chromatic typesetting: defined terms use a warmer ink so the eye picks up rhythm without underlines screaming. 3) Single intentional motion (hero Ken Burns) and zero scroll-driven effects — the article is read, not performed.

title: Order-book trading panel with bid/ask depth waterfall and price-locked column alignment
design_brief: You're building the order-book panel for a crypto exchange (think Coinbase Pro, Hyperliquid, Drift). Active traders stare at this for 8 hours a day. Pain point: most order books either misalign columns when prices change (jarring), or animate every update with a flash that becomes seizure-inducing during volatility. The bar: Hyperliquid's UI (the current gold standard), Bloomberg Terminal density without Bloomberg's hostility, Polymarket's elegance.
