anti_patterns_to_avoid: Flashing green/red on every price update — useless during 100 updates/sec
tabular-nums alone — doesn't handle decimal alignment for varying-precision numbers
Spring animations on price changes — adds phantom lag, breaks trust
Vertical centering of the spread row — should be a hard divider
Right-aligned numbers without grouping — '1234567' instead of '1,234,567'
Hover tooltips on every row showing 'Price: X, Size: Y' — already visible
ux_reasoning: An order book is two trends visualized as a snapshot. The traders need three things simultaneously: spot-check the spread, scan the depth distribution, and click-to-place at a specific price. Failing any of these makes the panel useless. Column alignment is the foundation. Prices align on the decimal point — not right-aligned, not left-aligned, decimal-aligned. We achieve this without JavaScript by splitting the number into integer + fractional parts and rendering them in fixed-width inline-blocks: 1,234.56. This is the Bloomberg move and it's why their numbers always look stable. Depth visualization: each row has a horizontal bar in the background showing cumulative depth, growing from the spread outward. For bids (below spread), the bar grows from right to left in a faint green; for asks (above spread), it grows from left to right in a faint red. This is the depth waterfall. Critically, opacity stays at 12% — visible at a glance but never competes with the numbers. We use linear-gradient on background, not a separate element, so it never desyncs from the row. Update animation: never a per-row flash. Instead, when the entire book updates (which happens at 10-50Hz), we use a single requestAnimationFrame batch. Rows that changed get a 1px left border in their side color for exactly one frame (16ms), then it's gone. The eye perceives motion without the panel becoming a strobe. The spread row is the visual anchor. It's the only row that has visible color (a thin horizontal line at half-pixel opacity in each direction), and it shows: current best bid, current best ask, spread in absolute and percentage, and last trade price + direction arrow. This is the moment of focus; everything else is context. Click-to-place: clicking a price prefills the order entry panel with that price. Shift+click prefills with size from that row too. This is the Hyperliquid move that turns the order book from 'display' into 'instrument'. Rejected: depth chart on the side as a curve. Tested it — traders found themselves looking at two visualizations of the same data. The horizontal in-row bars subsume the depth chart's job. Also rejected: showing depth as a heatmap with bucket aggregation. Loses too much precision; traders want each price level.

design_tokens.colors: Bids: oklch(0.65 0.15 145) at 12% alpha for depth bar, oklch(0.55 0.14 145) for prices. Asks: oklch(0.65 0.18 25) at 12% alpha for depth bar, oklch(0.58 0.16 25) for prices. Spread row separator: 0.5px solid oklch(0.5 0.005 250). Background: oklch(0.12 0.005 250) (almost black, slightly cool). Update flash border: same as bid/ask color, 1 frame only.
design_tokens.typography: All numbers: 'Berkeley Mono', 'JetBrains Mono', monospace, 12px / 16px. Tabular-nums + slashed-zero. NEVER mix sans/mono on number rows. Headers: 'Inter' 10px uppercase tracking-wider.
design_tokens.spacing: Row height: 16px (extreme density — Bloomberg-level). Column gaps: 8px. Container padding: 4px horizontal. Total visible rows in a 600px panel: ~32 + spread row.
design_tokens.motion: Update flash: 16ms (1 frame) — that's it. NO transitions on numbers. The depth bar updates instantly with the number update. Spread row arrows: no animation, just position change.

component_architecture: <OrderBookPanel<TLevel extends BookLevel> bids={[]} asks={[]} onPriceClick={...} />. Internally uses a useBookSync() hook that subscribes to a websocket and batches updates per rAF — React renders at 60Hz max regardless of feed rate. <PriceCell decimals={2} value={number} /> handles decimal alignment. <DepthBar side='bid'|'ask' fraction={number} /> renders the gradient background. The flash effect uses a CSS variable that's set/cleared imperatively (no React state change per row).
interaction_details: Click a price row: prefills order entry with that price. Shift+click: prefills price AND size.
Update at 50Hz: rows that changed get a 1-frame (16ms) left border in their side color. No flashing, no strobing.
Hover row: subtle bg shift. No tooltip — all info is already visible.
Spread row: shows last trade direction with a unicode arrow + spread absolute/percentage. The only animated change here is value text — never a flash.
Keyboard: Tab into book, j/k to navigate rows, Enter on a row prefills order at that price.
accessibility_considerations: Each row is a real button with aria-label encoding side + size + price ('bid 0.5 BTC at 67,420.50'). Despite the visual density, screen readers traverse the structure as a list of actionable levels. role='separator' on the spread row. Reduced-motion: no change needed — the 1-frame flash is already imperceptible, and depth bars never animate. Color is supplemented by position (bids below spread, asks above) and the side-encoded number color. Contrast: side colors meet 4.5:1 against the dark bg.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Decimal-aligned prices via integer/fractional split — numbers stay rock-stable as values change, the Bloomberg move. 2) In-row depth bars at 12% opacity replace a separate depth chart while staying perfectly synchronized with the row. 3) Update 'flash' is a 1-frame (16ms) imperceptible cue, not a 200ms color pulse — eye-perceivable change without strobing during volatility.

title: Vim-modal file tree with relative-line-number jumps and visual-line selection
design_brief: You're building the file tree for a developer-facing IDE in the browser (think VS Code Web, Replit, StackBlitz, GitHub.dev). Pain point: every web IDE ships a file tree that requires the mouse — chevrons to expand, double-click to open. The bar: real Vim/Helix users editing project trees from keyboard, NERDTree's interaction model adapted thoughtfully for web, Zed's file tree.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tab to focus tree + arrow keys is the entire keyboard story
Tooltip showing 'Press F2 to rename' on every hover
Expand all/collapse all in a context menu — power users want individual control
Wrapping tree rows so long names create vertical layout shifts
File icons from a generic icon library (folder/file/code) — adds noise without info
Right-pad with action icons that appear on hover row (delete, rename) — competes with focus
ux_reasoning:
A vim-modal file tree is not 'a tree with shortcuts'. It's a stateful navigation system where modes change what every key does, and visual feedback for the current mode is non-negotiable.

Three modes: NORMAL (default — j/k to navigate, h/l to collapse/expand, o to open), VISUAL (V to enter — selection extends with j/k, d deletes selection, y yanks paths to clipboard), and SEARCH (/ to enter — type to fuzzy filter the tree, Enter opens, Esc returns to NORMAL with filter applied).

Relative line numbers: when enabled (rnu in vim), the focused row shows '0' and others show distance from focus. Type '5j' to jump down 5 rows. This is a divisive feature — we hide it behind a toggle in the tree header, default off. Power users will find it.

The visual mode is the killer feature. Press V on a file; it becomes the anchor. j extends selection downward, k upward. y copies file paths newline-separated to clipboard. d shows a confirm prompt with the count ('Delete 4 files?'). This is yank-and-delete from vim, which most users have muscle memory for. Holding shift extends selection during normal mouse-click too (the standard interaction), so newcomers aren't punished.

Filtering with / uses fuzzy match against full paths (so 'usrt' matches 'src/users/types.ts'). Filter dims non-matching rows to 30% opacity instead of hiding them — preserves spatial memory ('that file was below this folder'). This is the Tree-View-Like-Sublime move.

Mode indicator: a single character in the bottom-right of the tree header, in a colored chip — 'N' (gray), 'V' (orange), '/' (blue). Tiny, but absolute clarity about current mode. Without this, users get confused; with it, the entire interaction self-teaches.

Motion: zero. File trees with animated expansion become annoying after 5 expansions. Expand is instant. The only motion is the mode chip changing color (80ms).

Rejected: a vim emulator that supports all of vim's commands. Scope creep nightmare; we ship 12 commands and they're chosen because each replaces a common mouse action. Also rejected: 'normal mode' as the only mode (no V or /). Tested — power users got bored without expression. Modes are how expressiveness scales.

design_tokens.colors: Tree bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250). Row text: oklch(0.85 0.005 250). Focused row bg: oklch(0.28 0.01 250). Visual-selected row bg: oklch(0.35 0.06 50). Mode chip: NORMAL oklch(0.5 0.01 250), VISUAL oklch(0.6 0.16 50), SEARCH oklch(0.55 0.16 250). Filter-dimmed rows: 30% opacity.
design_tokens.typography: Tree rows: 'JetBrains Mono' 12px / 18px — monospace because filenames often contain numbers / extensions that benefit. Mode chip: 11px uppercase tracking-wider. Relative line numbers: tabular-nums oklch(0.45 0.005 250).
design_tokens.spacing: Row height: 22px. Indent per level: 12px (tighter than typical). Tree padding: 4px. Icon width: 16px (only file-type icon, no folder/file generic).
design_tokens.motion: Mode chip color change: 80ms ease-out. No expand animation. No selection animation. Filter dim transition: 100ms ease-out opacity.

component_architecture: <FileTree<TNode extends TreeNode>> generic over node shape. Tree state: focused path, expanded set, mode, visual anchor, filter query, count buffer (for '5j'). Mode-driven keyboard handler is a single function that switches on mode + key. State lives in a TreeModeContext (since multiple components need it: header chip, rows, search input). Filter is applied as a visual filter (CSS opacity) not a data filter (preserves layout).
interaction_details: NORMAL: j/k navigate, h collapses/parent, l expands/opens, o opens file, / enters SEARCH, V enters VISUAL. Numeric prefix supported: 5j jumps 5 down.
VISUAL: anchor set on entry; j/k extend selection; y yanks paths to clipboard; d prompts delete-confirm with count; Esc returns to NORMAL.
SEARCH: typing filters tree by fuzzy match (preserves layout, dims non-matches to 30%); Enter opens current focus + returns to NORMAL with filter retained; Esc cancels.
Mode chip in header changes color (gray→orange→blue) over 80ms — the absolute mode indicator.
Relative line numbers (off by default): when on, focused row shows 0 and others show distance — enables count-prefix muscle memory.
accessibility_considerations: role='tree' on container, role='treeitem' on rows with aria-selected and aria-expanded. The whole tree is a single tab stop (one tabIndex), then arrow navigation inside — the standard WAI-ARIA tree pattern. Mode state is exposed via aria-label on the chip (e.g., 'Visual selection mode'). Search input is a real input with proper label. Reduced-motion: mode chip transition is the only animation; it's only 80ms and color-only (no motion), so no special handling needed. Color is supplemented by mode letter ('N' / 'V' / '/'). Contrast: row text at 7:1 on bg, focused row at 5:1.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Three-mode interaction (NORMAL/VISUAL/SEARCH) with a single-character mode chip — vim's expressiveness without vim's onboarding cliff. 2) Visual mode supports y (yank to clipboard) and d (delete with confirm) — real bulk operations from keyboard, not just navigation. 3) Filter dims non-matches to 30% instead of hiding — preserves spatial memory of file locations during fuzzy search.

title: Multi-user form editing with soft field locks and last-writer-wins reconciliation
design_brief: You're building a collaborative form for an enterprise contract editor (think DocuSign's multi-party form + Notion's database collab + Pitch's slide co-editing). Five lawyers from two firms editing the same purchase agreement at the same time. Pain point: most multi-user forms either lock entire documents (too heavy) or have no concept of field-level conflicts (everyone overwrites everyone). The bar: Figma's text field collab, Notion's table cell collab, Linear's collaborative issue editor.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Document-level locks that show 'Alice is editing this contract'
Hard field locks that block typing entirely with a 'wait for Bob to finish' message
Last-writer-wins with no visual indication that someone else's edit was overwritten
Conflict resolution modals that interrupt typing flow
Generic 'someone is here' avatar stack with no context
Real-time updates that jump the cursor when remote text inserts above
ux_reasoning:
Field-level collaboration on structured forms is fundamentally different from doc collaboration (which uses CRDTs). Form fields have semantic identity — Field A is 'effective_date', Field B is 'consideration_amount' — so conflict resolution can be per-field and per-semantic.

The model is SOFT locks: when a user focuses a field, others see a colored ring around the field (in that user's color) with their name as a small chip at the field corner. Others CAN still type into the field — but they get a warning glyph + the chip turns to 'Alice typing here' in a stronger color. On blur, the last writer wins, but a 'changed by Alice 3s ago' indicator appears on the field for 8s so the user notices and can undo.

This is NOT optimistic — it's pragmatic. Hard locks frustrate real teams ('I just want to fix this typo, why is it locked?'). No locks at all destroys data. Soft locks + visible last-writer + undo affordance gives users agency.

Cursor preservation during remote inserts: when Alice types into a field while Bob's cursor is positioned later in the same field, naive replacement jumps Bob's cursor. We use the field's contentEditable selection API to translate Bob's cursor offset through the remote operation — same technique as ProseMirror's collab module, but simpler because we're doing it for individual fields not whole docs.

Presence indicator on each field: a small 8px colored ring on the field's right side when someone is focused there. Hover shows the user's name + how long they've been on this field. The ring color is the user's brand color. We deliberately don't use avatars on every field (would clutter); we use a colored ring + a single avatar chip on the document header that shows all currently-present users.

Field history: each field stores its last 3 changes (timestamp, author, value). Right-clicking the field shows a small popover: 'Last edits: Bob 2m ago changed from "30 days" to "45 days". Alice 5m ago changed from "" to "30 days". Revert to →'. This is Linear's edit history applied to form fields.

Rejected: a complete CRDT for the form. Overkill — forms have semantic field identity that CRDTs are designed to discover; we already know it. Last-writer-wins per field with visible attribution and undo affordance is the right granularity. Also rejected: requiring user confirmation before allowing typing into a focused-by-other field. Friction destroys flow.

design_tokens.colors: Field focus ring (self): oklch(0.55 0.18 250) 2px. Field focus ring (other user): user's color at 2px, default opacity 0.5; jumps to 1.0 when they're actively typing. 'Changed by Alice' label: 11px, user color text on transparent bg. User-color palette: same 12-color from cursors. Header avatar stack: 24px circles with 2px overlap.
design_tokens.typography: Field label: Inter 13px medium. Field value: 14px regular. User chip on field: 10px medium. 'Changed 3s ago' indicator: 11px regular oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Field height: 36px. User chip floats at top-right of field, -8px margin. Avatar stack in header: 4px gap.
design_tokens.motion: Field ring fade in/out: 150ms ease-out. Typing-intensity ring color shift: 200ms ease-out. 'Changed by' indicator: 200ms fade-in, holds 8s, 300ms fade-out. NO animations on text content.

component_architecture: <CollaborativeField<TValue>> generic over value type. Props: { id, value, onChange, label }. Subscribes to a PresenceContext for per-field focus state and a HistoryContext for last-N-edits. Uses a useTransform() hook to translate local selection through remote operations. Last-writer-wins handled by the network layer; the component just renders state and history. <PresenceContext /> aggregates all currently-present users from a websocket subscription.
interaction_details: User A focuses a field: 2px ring in user A's color appears around the field for everyone else, plus a small 'A' chip at field corner.
User A types: ring intensity jumps to full opacity, chip turns to 'A typing'. Stops typing for 2s: ring fades to 50% opacity, chip returns to just the name.
User B types in same field while A is there: A's typing is replaced character-by-character; B's chip overlays. Last writer's value wins at network layer.
User A's value is overwritten by B: A sees 'Changed by B' line below field in B's color for 8s, with an 'Undo' link that reverts to A's previous value.
Right-click any field: small popover shows last 3 edits with author + timestamp, click any prior value to revert.
accessibility_considerations: Presence chips include user names (not just color), so screen reader users hear 'Alice is typing' via aria-live. Recent-remote-change row uses role='status' aria-live='polite'. Field labels are real <label htmlFor>. Color is supplemented by the name chip and the explicit text 'Changed by Alice'. Keyboard: Cmd+Z while focused on a field undoes the LAST remote change (not just local typing), with announcement. Reduced-motion: ring color transitions become instant; 'Changed by' indicator appears/disappears without fade. Contrast: user color rings tested against light field bg for 3:1 minimum.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Soft locks (visible focus rings) instead of hard locks — users always retain agency, see who's editing, and can undo conflicts. 2) Cursor offset translation through remote operations keeps the local cursor stable when remote text changes the field length. 3) Per-field 3-edit history with right-click revert — fine-grained collaboration undo without leaving context.

title: Sustained-press 'super-like' with charge buildup and energy release
design_brief: You're building a reaction interaction for a community platform (think Discord reactions + Apple Music's love + LinkedIn Reactions + Pieter Levels' Twitter). Pain point: most like buttons are binary — click increments a counter, animate a heart. Boring; doesn't reward intent. The bar: Twitter's hold-to-super-like, Apple's force-touch 3D Touch quick actions, Instagram's double-tap heart.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Heart that scales to 1.2 and back on click — every product does this
Confetti burst on first like (loses meaning by like 10)
Long-press menu with 6 reaction emojis that takes 400ms to appear
Spring-back-with-overshoot scale animation that everyone uses
Number counter that flips like a slot machine
Disabled state after liking that prevents un-liking
ux_reasoning:
A reaction button is small but high-volume — users click it dozens of times per session. Every animation choice gets weighted by 100x. The interaction should be CHARGEABLE: a regular click is a regular like, a held click charges toward a super-like, and releasing mid-charge cancels.

Mechanics: press-and-hold for 250ms+ enters 'charge mode' — the button starts filling with a radial gradient from center outward, and a faint ring of particles emanates from the perimeter. Release before 100% charge: cancels (no like recorded). Release at 100%+ charge: records a super-like (worth 5x in algorithm; rare; precious). A regular click (down + up within 150ms) records a normal like.

Visual feedback during charge: button color shifts from neutral to brand-warm over the charge period via a conic-gradient that fills clockwise. At 100% charge: a single 'release' moment where the button briefly halos with a soft 16px blur shadow and a single particle burst (8 particles, NOT 50; restraint is the move). The number ticks up by 5 (the super-like value) with a single subtle slide-up animation on the digits.

The number animation is the key. Most counters either pop with scale (cheap) or do a slot-machine flip (annoying). We do a 'paper tape' effect: the old digit slides up and out, the new digit slides in from below over 200ms with a slight 4px overshoot — only on the digits that actually changed, not the whole number. This is the iOS Apple Watch activity ring counter, transposed.

Un-charge logic: holding without releasing for 1500ms+ (way past the charge threshold) does NOT fire repeatedly. The interaction is single-shot per gesture. If the user wants to add another like, they release and press again. This prevents 'I held it too long and bought 200 likes' regret moments.

Haptic: a single firm tap at 100% charge (when the super-like becomes available). NOT one at the start of charge, NOT one on release, NOT one per particle. Most haptic-heavy interactions are infuriating; minimalism here is respect.

Mobile gestures: same charge mechanic on long-press. Double-tap on the button (within 300ms) fires a super-like directly (skipping the charge UI) — power users who know want a shortcut.

Rejected: a long-press menu of 6 reaction emojis (Slack/Discord pattern). It works there because Slack reactions are a richer comm primitive; for a single-stream feed, the cognitive cost of choosing one of 6 emojis at every reaction is too high. Single primary action + a 'super' modifier is the right scope. Also rejected: confetti. Confetti is a 'first time you do anything' animation; on like #50, it's debt.

design_tokens.colors: Default: oklch(0.6 0.005 250). Hover: oklch(0.5 0.005 250). Charging fill: conic-gradient from oklch(0.65 0.18 25) (warm). Super-like halo: oklch(0.65 0.18 25 / 0.4) at 16px blur. Counter neutral: oklch(0.55 0.005 250). Counter +super: oklch(0.65 0.18 25).
design_tokens.typography: Counter: Inter 13px tabular-nums medium. Super-like 'thanks' caption (post-fire): 11px regular for 1500ms.
design_tokens.spacing: Button: 36px square. Counter: 24px gap from button. Particle perimeter: 6 particles at 16px radius from button center.
design_tokens.motion: Charge duration to 100%: 1000ms. Charge fill: conic-gradient mask, real-time. Release at 100%: 120ms halo flash + 200ms paper-tape counter. Particle burst: 8 particles over 400ms with cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1) ease-out, opacity fade to 0.

component_architecture: <ReactionButton<TKind extends string>> generic over kinds (could expand to other reactions later). Takes count, onLike(kind: 'regular' | 'super'), and currentUserDid (so the button shows liked state). Internally tracks press-start-time and charge-progress as refs (not state — would rerender at 60Hz). Renders a base button + an overlay <ChargeFill> + <ParticleBurst trigger={...}>. <AnimatedCount> handles the paper-tape effect for the number using FLIP.
interaction_details: Quick click (down+up <150ms): regular like, counter ticks up by 1 via paper-tape.
Press and hold 250ms+: charge mode begins — conic gradient fills clockwise as charge progresses over 1000ms.
Release before 100%: nothing recorded (cancel). Release at 100%: super-like recorded (+5), halo flashes 600ms, 8 particles burst outward over 400ms, single haptic tap.
Double-tap (within 300ms): immediate super-like, skipping charge UI — power user shortcut.
Already liked: same button, single click un-likes. Counter ticks down.
accessibility_considerations: aria-label clearly describes both interactions ('Like. Hold to super-like'). aria-pressed indicates current state. Counter has aria-live='polite' so screen reader users hear count changes — but debounced via the AnimatedCount component to avoid spam during rapid clicks. Keyboard: Enter = regular like; holding Enter for 1s = super-like (key repeat detection on first 'keydown' only). Reduced-motion: paper-tape and particle burst disabled, halo flash becomes a brief opacity blink only. Color is supplemented by the heart fill state (outline vs filled) and counter color. Haptic always optional.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Charge mechanic gives users two distinct expressions of intent (regular vs super) from one button, with visible buildup that lets them cancel mid-gesture. 2) Paper-tape per-digit counter animation only animates the digits that actually changed — never the whole number, never a slot-machine. 3) Single haptic tap at 100% charge (not at start, release, or per-particle) — restraint as a feature.

title: Conversion funnel with stacked segment breakdown and drop-off step explanation
design_brief: You're building the funnel visualization for a product analytics tool (think Mixpanel + Amplitude + Posthog). PMs use this to diagnose where users drop off. Pain point: standard funnel charts show declining horizontal bars with percentages, but tell you NOTHING about WHY users dropped — and they don't let you decompose drop-off by user segment in-place. The bar: Posthog's funnel chart, Amplitude's funnel insights, June.so's funnel view.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Horizontal bar chart with decreasing widths and percentage labels
Hover tooltip that just repeats the bar's value
Generic 'compare segments' as a separate chart below
Drop-off rate calculated only between adjacent steps (misses the absolute pool view)
Animating funnel bars to grow on mount with a stagger — annoying after the second view
Generic empty state when funnel has 0 conversions
ux_reasoning:
A funnel is a SEQUENCE of bottlenecks. The interesting question is never 'what's the conversion rate?' — it's 'which segment dropped off at which step, and is that drop-off normal?'. The viz must answer both at a glance.

Layout: vertical funnel (not horizontal). Vertical scales better for 8+ step funnels, and stacked segments per step are easier to read vertically. Each step is a horizontal bar where the WIDTH is the number of users at that step, and the bar is divided into colored segments showing breakdown (by source, by plan tier, or by any selected dimension). Between steps: a 'drop-off arrow' showing what percentage left, with the actual count when hovered.

The segment colors are stable across steps — 'Free plan' is always the same blue, 'Pro' the same orange. This is critical for visual continuity; you can SEE that Pro users dominate step 1 but free users dominate step 4, just by color shift.

Drop-off explanation: between each step, we render a small 'explanation card' inline (collapsed by default, click to expand) that lists the top 3 reasons users dropped — pulled from a metadata source (e.g., 'session expired', 'redirected to login', 'tab closed'). This is the killer move: most funnel viz tools tell you THAT users dropped off; this one shows WHY.

Interaction: clicking a segment of any bar filters the entire funnel to just that segment (so you can see how 'Pro signups from Twitter' specifically funneled). The filter applies optimistically. Clicking again removes filter. Multiple filters stack with AND.

Motion: the funnel renders with NO entry animation. Funnel views are scrutinized; users want to read them, not watch them. The only motion: when a filter is applied, the bars resize over 250ms with the choreography that preserves the user's eye on the just-clicked segment (a FLIP technique).

Rejected: a Sankey diagram for the same data. Sankeys are beautiful but unreadable beyond 3 levels and 5 segments. Vertical stacked bars scale to 12+ steps and 8+ segments without losing legibility. Also rejected: animating the drop-off percentage counting up. It's a number; let users read it.

design_tokens.colors: Segment palette: 8 colors from the same cohort palette used elsewhere, OKLCH for perceptual uniformity. Bar bg (the 'pool' visualization): oklch(0.96 0.005 250). Drop-off arrow: oklch(0.55 0.005 250). Filter active state: 2px outline in segment color.
design_tokens.typography: Step name: Inter 13px medium. Count + percentage: 12px tabular-nums. Drop-off percentage: 11px italic regular. Explanation card text: 12px regular oklch(0.45).
design_tokens.spacing: Bar height: 32px. Gap between bars: 16px. Bar max-width: 480px (so a 10-step funnel fits in viewport). Explanation card: 8px above the next bar.
design_tokens.motion: Filter applied: 250ms FLIP-driven resize, cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). NO mount animation. Hover segment: 80ms opacity shift on adjacent segments (focus is on the hovered one).

component_architecture: <FunnelChart<TStep extends FunnelStep>> generic. Each step has { id, name, count, segments: { id, color, count }[], dropOffReasons?: string[] }. <FunnelStep /> renders one bar with <Segment /> children. Filter state is held in <FunnelChart> root and passed down. Resize choreography uses a useFlip() hook that captures bounding boxes before and after a state change, then applies inverse transforms with a transition.
interaction_details: Hover a segment: adjacent segments dim 20%, hovered stays full opacity. Tooltip shows segment label + count + percentage-of-step (with comparison to step 1).
Click a segment: entire funnel filters to that segment. Bars resize over 250ms with choreographed FLIP — the clicked segment stays under your cursor.
Click drop-off arrow: expands to show top 3 reasons (e.g., '54% session expired'). Click again collapses.
Filter active: pill at top shows current filter. Click pill X to clear. Multiple filters stack with AND.
Segment colors are stable across steps — 'Pro plan' is always orange, so visual continuity is preserved across the entire funnel.
accessibility_considerations: Each segment is a real <button> with descriptive aria-label including label + count + filter status. The funnel structure is conveyed in DOM order (step 1 → step 2 → ...). Drop-off arrows are real buttons with aria-expanded for the explanation disclosure. Reduced-motion: FLIP resize becomes instant; segment dim-on-hover becomes immediate. Color is supplemented: filter pill includes segment label text; tooltip names the segment by label not color. Contrast: segment colors meet 3:1 against the light pool background.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Vertical funnel with stacked colored segments per step + stable colors across steps — visual continuity of cohorts as they drop off. 2) Inline drop-off explanation between each step shows WHY users left, not just HOW MANY — the diagnostic answer most funnels never provide. 3) Click-segment-to-filter with FLIP-choreographed resize keeps the user's eye on the segment they just clicked.

title: Sample-workspace clone with realistic data and 'replace as you go' migration
design_brief: You're building first-time onboarding for a project management tool (think Linear's sample workspace + Notion's templates + Height's getting-started). Pain point: most onboardings drop users into an empty app with a 'Create your first project' modal, or load them up with a template that has Lorem ipsum text. The bar: Linear's sample workspace (the gold standard — you arrive into a populated app with realistic issues you can interact with immediately), Things 3's sample data, Notion's templates that feel real.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Empty state with 'Create your first project' button and nothing else
Template gallery modal as first screen
Sample data with placeholder text ('Item 1', 'Lorem ipsum')
Sample data clearly labeled '[Sample]' on every item, screaming 'this is fake'
Forced 5-step setup before user can touch the actual product
No way to delete sample data without finding it in a settings panel
ux_reasoning:
The first 30 seconds determine whether the user understands the product. An empty app teaches nothing; a modal tour bores. A SAMPLE WORKSPACE — a fully populated, realistically-shaped instance the user can explore and gradually replace — is the only humane onboarding.

Mechanics: on signup, we clone a hand-curated sample workspace into the user's account. It contains a mock team ('Spruce, Maple, Aspen'), realistic issues with prose titles ('Investigate flaky test in checkout E2E suite'), a backlog with priorities, in-progress issues with comment threads, completed issues with shipped-stamps. Everything looks like a real workspace at week 8.

The disambiguation between sample and real is SUBTLE, not loud. Sample issues are tagged with a small flag icon (4x4px) in their corner — visible if you look, invisible if you don't. No banners, no '[Sample]' prefixes, no watermarks. The user shouldn't have to mentally filter sample from real; they should be able to USE the sample data as starting material.

'Replace as you go' is the killer move. When the user creates their first real issue, we don't ask 'do you want to delete the sample data?'. We track sample-issue interactions: if a user opens a sample issue and edits its title, it's no longer sample — it's a real issue they adopted. If they delete a sample issue, it's gone. After 7 days, any untouched sample issues are auto-archived (NOT deleted) with a one-time note in the sidebar 'Sample issues archived — restore from settings'. This respects the user's pace.

Contextual hints within the sample data: each sample issue has a small italic 'note' field (e.g., 'Tip: drag this to a different project') that's invisible unless the user hovers the issue. So the sample data ITSELF teaches features without modal overlays.

Workspace name: 'My Workspace' default; if the user is from a known company domain, we pre-fill 'Acme Co'. The user can rename it whenever. We don't ask in onboarding.

Rejected: forcing the user to pick a 'template' before entering the app. Adds friction, requires them to know what they want before they've seen the product. Sample workspace + freedom to delete is strictly better. Also rejected: a 'Welcome' issue from the founder as the first item. Feels cheesy; users skip it.

design_tokens.colors: Sample flag icon: oklch(0.7 0.1 60) at 60% opacity — visible but not aggressive. 'Adopted from sample' transient indicator: oklch(0.55 0.14 145) for 800ms then fades. Sample-archived notice: oklch(0.96 0.005 250) bg, regular type color.
design_tokens.typography: Sample issue content uses the exact same type ramp as real content. The italic 'tip' note inside sample issues: 11px italic oklch(0.55 0.005 250) — easily distinguishable as meta, never confused with content.
design_tokens.spacing: Sample flag icon: 6px from issue title bottom-right, 4px square. Adopted-from-sample indicator: appears next to issue ID for 800ms then fades.
design_tokens.motion: Sample flag fade-out after adoption: 400ms ease-out opacity. Adopted-from-sample indicator slide-in: 200ms ease-out, hold 600ms, fade out 200ms. Sample archival: no animation; happens silently in background.

component_architecture: <SampleWorkspaceCloner /> runs once at signup; uses a curated JSON template and writes to the user's DB. Each issue carries an isSample boolean + adoptionLevel: 'untouched' | 'opened' | 'edited' | 'adopted'. <IssueRow /> reads adoptionLevel and shows the flag for 'untouched'/'opened', hides for 'edited'/'adopted'. <SampleArchivalCron /> runs daily checking for sample issues untouched >7d and moves them to archive with a workspace-level note.
interaction_details: Sign up: sample workspace clones in <500ms; user lands on a populated issue list, not an empty state.
Hover a sample issue: a small italic tip floats below ('Press C to create a new one') — invisible until hovered, gone on mouseleave.
Click sample issue: opens detail; adoptionLevel transitions 'untouched' → 'opened' (still tagged as sample).
Edit the title or body: a 'Adopted' indicator briefly shows next to the issue ID; the sample flag fades out over 400ms; the issue is now real.
7 days later: untouched sample issues are silently moved to an Archive view; a single non-modal notice appears in the sidebar bottom: 'Sample issues archived. Restore →'.
accessibility_considerations: Sample flag has aria-label='Sample issue' and a title attribute on hover for sighted users. Tip text appears as a proper role='tooltip'. No announcements about sample data to screen reader users — would be just as noisy for them. Sample issues are full participants in the keyboard navigation flow (j/k navigates them like real issues). Reduced-motion: 'Adopted' indicator appears and disappears instantly. Color: sample flag color is supplementary; the title attribute conveys the same information verbally.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Sample workspace is a fully populated, realistic team workspace — not a tutorial mode or template gallery. The user lands in week-8 product, not week-1 emptiness. 2) 'Adopted on edit' means the user converts samples to real issues just by using them — no 'do you want to keep this?' modal. 3) Sample issues silently archive after 7 days untouched, with a single non-modal notice — respects the user's pace without leaving cruft.

title: Calendar date-range picker with smart presets and keyboard-driven typing input
design_brief: You're building the date-range picker for an analytics tool (think Vercel's date range, Posthog's date picker, Linear's filter dates, Datadog's time picker). Power users want both: a calendar grid for visual selection AND a typed natural-language input ('last 30 days', 'q1 2024'). Pain point: most pickers force one mode (calendar OR input). The bar: Linear's date filter, GitHub's date range, Mode Analytics' time picker.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Two separate inputs for start/end with no range visualization
Calendar with only one month visible at a time for range selection
Disabled 'Apply' button while a range is being constructed
Snap-to-week / snap-to-month modes hidden behind a settings cog
Time-zone selector buried in advanced settings
Generic 'Today' button that doesn't update if the user has another preset selected
ux_reasoning:
A date-range picker is a small UI that's surprisingly central — analysts use it dozens of times per session. The two-mode tension (calendar vs typed) is real: typed is faster for known ranges ('last 7 days'), calendar is faster for exploratory or specific selections. The picker should support both seamlessly.

Layout: a two-month calendar side-by-side (current month + next), with a left rail of preset buttons ('Today', 'Yesterday', 'Last 7 days', 'Last 30 days', 'Quarter to date', 'Year to date', 'Custom...'). The top of the picker has a single input that accepts BOTH natural language ('last 30 days', 'jan 1 to mar 31') AND a typed range ('2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31'). Typing into the input drives the calendar selection in real-time.

The parser is the killer feature. It accepts: 'today', 'yesterday', 'last N days', 'last N weeks', 'this month', 'last month', 'q1', 'q1 2024', 'jan to mar', '2024-01-01', '2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31', '2024' (full year), 'mar 15' (current year implied). On every keystroke, we parse + update the calendar visualization. Unparseable input shows a subtle 1px red bottom border (no error message until blur — typing is fluid).

Calendar interaction: click a day to set start, click another day to set end. Hovering after start-set previews the range. Cmd+click to select the same day as both start and end (single-day range). Shift+arrow keys on the focused day extends the range. The visualization fills cells with a subtle bg in the range and rounds the start + end cells.

Presets are smart: the rail highlights the preset that matches the current selection. So if the user manually picks Jan 1 to today, 'Year to date' becomes the highlighted preset — auto-discovery of named ranges.

Timezone: a tiny chip at the bottom shows the timezone the range is interpreted in ('America/New_York'). Click to change. Defaults to user's browser zone. Most picker tools hide this and break globally distributed teams.

Apply: there's no Apply button. The selection commits on blur (closing the picker) or on Enter (after a typed input). This is the Linear model — friction is the enemy.

Rejected: a slider for picking ranges. Beautiful for time-of-day, terrible for date ranges that span months. Also rejected: requiring a 'compare to' second range built-in. Add it as an opt-in toggle later — most users want a single range.

design_tokens.colors: Range fill: oklch(0.92 0.05 250) light / oklch(0.3 0.04 250) dark. Range endpoints: oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Today indicator: 1px ring oklch(0.55 0.18 250). Preset active: oklch(0.94 0.01 250) bg + oklch(0.2 0.01 250) text. Parser error border: oklch(0.6 0.18 25) bottom-1px.
design_tokens.typography: Input: Inter 14px regular. Calendar day numbers: Inter 13px tabular-nums. Day-of-week headers: 11px uppercase tracking-wider. Preset buttons: 13px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Calendar cell: 32px square. Two months side-by-side: 16px gap. Preset rail: 140px wide. Total picker width: ~620px.
design_tokens.motion: Range hover preview: 80ms ease-out bg fill transition. Picker open: 120ms opacity + 4px translateY. No spring.

component_architecture: <DateRangePicker value={[start, end] | null} onChange={...} timezone='auto'>. Internal state: pending start, pending end, input string, parser result. Uses a useParseDateRange() hook that returns { range | null, error }. Calendar grid is generic over 2-month layout. Preset list reads current selection and computes 'is this preset active' for highlight. No Apply button — onChange fires on commit (blur or Enter).
interaction_details: Type 'last 30 days' → calendar instantly highlights the last 30 days; preset rail highlights 'Last 30 days'. Press Enter or click outside: range committed.
Click a day in the calendar → first click sets start (single highlighted cell); moving mouse over other days previews range; second click commits range.
Type 'q1 2024' → highlights Jan 1 to Mar 31 of 2024. Type 'q1' alone → uses current year.
Manually pick Jan 1 to today → 'Month to date' preset auto-highlights (smart preset detection).
Invalid input (e.g., 'last yesterday') → input bottom border becomes red; no error message until blur (typing flow preserved).
accessibility_considerations: Real <button> for each calendar cell with aria-label as full date string ('Tuesday, January 23, 2024'). Input has aria-invalid bound to parser error. Picker is role='dialog' with aria-label. Keyboard: arrow keys move focus between days; Shift+arrow extends range; Enter commits; Esc closes without committing. Preset buttons have aria-pressed indicating active preset. Timezone chip is a button with aria-label='Change timezone'. Reduced-motion: open animation disabled. Color: range fill is supplemented by start/end cells having different shape (rounded fill); active preset has font-weight change.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Single input accepts both ISO ('2024-01-01 to 2024-03-31') AND natural language ('last 7 days', 'q1 2024') — speed for power users, accessibility for everyone. 2) Smart preset detection: manually selecting a range that matches a named preset auto-highlights it — discovery of names without learning the names. 3) No Apply button — commits on blur or Enter, respecting the user's flow.

title: Navigable 3D dependency graph for a microservice topology with focus-driven LOD
design_brief: You're building the architecture map view for a platform engineering tool (think Backstage's catalog + Pixie + a 3D version of Honeycomb's service map). SREs use this during incidents to trace dependencies. Pain point: 2D dependency graphs become spaghetti past ~30 nodes, and existing 3D graphs (like Cytoscape.js with WebGL) are slow and disorienting. The bar: Apple's Maps 3D mode, NASA's Eyes on the Solar System, Observable Plot for inspiration on focus-driven layouts.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Force-directed layout running every frame — wobbles forever, hurts to look at
Edge rendering as straight lines through other nodes — visual chaos
Generic 'click to expand neighborhood' that opens a modal
Loading all 800 nodes at full detail — kills performance
Spinning the camera continuously to 'show it's 3D' — vertigo
Black background with rainbow nodes — looks like a 2009 demo
ux_reasoning:
3D for graphs only earns its weight if it solves a 2D problem. The problem 2D can't solve: when 200+ services have cross-cutting dependencies, edges overlap into a hairball regardless of layout. 3D lets us route edges through space without crossings — the original use case for 3D graphs (network topology) is still its best one.

Layout: hierarchical 3D — services organized by 'tier' on the Y axis (frontend at top, backend middle, datastores bottom). Within a tier, nodes are placed using a force-directed pre-computation (NOT continuously running — computed once, frozen). Edges curve in 3D Bezier paths between nodes, avoiding intersection with other nodes via a routing pass.

Level-of-detail (LOD): the camera distance to a node controls its render fidelity. Far: a billboard quad with the service's status color. Medium: a 3D box with name label. Close: full node detail (recent error count, latency sparkline mounted as an HTML overlay). This is the key performance win — 800 nodes are renderable because 780 are billboard quads at any moment.

Focus interaction: clicking a node smoothly orbits the camera to frame it (700ms with cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)), and the node enters its detail LOD. Adjacent nodes (direct dependencies) stay at medium LOD; everything 2+ hops away dims to 40% opacity. This is the 'pinned attention' pattern — when you focus, the world recedes.

During an incident, services with error rates above threshold pulse with a slow (2s) glow in their status color (red/yellow). The pulse is subtle — additive blending on a billboard sprite, not flashing geometry.

Navigation: drag to orbit, scroll to zoom (with smoothing), hold space to pan. Double-click empty space resets camera to overview. Press F when a node is focused to 'frame' it. We deliberately don't use first-person navigation (WASD) — analysts aren't gamers.

Performance: instanced meshes for billboards (single draw call for all far nodes). Render on demand (frameloop='demand'). Edges culled when camera is below a zoom threshold (they're noise at that distance). DPR capped at 1.5 on retina to avoid 4K+ resolutions.

Rejected: VR mode. Cool demo, real users won't put on a headset. Also rejected: WebXR exit affordance (none required since we don't enter XR).

design_tokens.colors: Service status: green oklch(0.65 0.14 145), yellow oklch(0.75 0.16 80), red oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Tier bands (faint horizontal planes): oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.05). Background: vertical gradient oklch(0.14 0.01 250) (top) → oklch(0.08 0.005 250) (bottom). Edge: oklch(0.5 0.005 250 / 0.4) — thin and not screaming.
design_tokens.typography: Node labels: SF Pro / Inter 12px, rendered as canvas textures or HTML overlays at medium-close LOD. Status tag: 10px uppercase tracking-wider. Detail HTML overlay: same as the rest of the app.
design_tokens.spacing: Tier spacing: 8 units. Inter-node minimum distance within tier: 1.5 units. Camera default position: (0, 4, 12).
design_tokens.motion: Focus orbit: 700ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1). Pulse for unhealthy services: 2000ms ease-in-out infinite, opacity 0.7 → 1.0 → 0.7 on additive sprite. Dim transitions on focus: 300ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <TopologyMap<TNode extends ServiceNode>> generic over node shape. <Canvas frameloop='demand'> with <Tier /> components (one per tier band) and <Nodes /> using instanced meshes for billboards + <DetailNode /> for the focused node and its neighbors. <FocusCamera focusId={...} /> reads from FocusContext and animates the camera. <NodeOverlay /> renders HTML overlays at-position for close-LOD nodes via the @react-three/drei Html component.
interaction_details: Drag: orbit camera around center. Scroll: zoom (smoothed). Hold space + drag: pan.
Click a node: camera orbits to frame it over 700ms, node enters full-detail LOD (HTML overlay with name/status), direct neighbors stay medium-detail, everything else dims to 40%.
Press F when a node is focused: re-frames it (in case the user has orbited away). Double-click empty space: resets camera to overview.
Unhealthy services (yellow/red): subtle pulse on additive sprite at 2s period — visible peripherally without being a strobe.
Far nodes render as billboard quads via instanced mesh — 800 nodes, single draw call for the far set.
accessibility_considerations: Canvas has aria-label describing it as a 3D service topology. A parallel <ul> with all services + statuses lives in the DOM (visually hidden but keyboard-navigable) — screen reader users get the same info via list. Pressing Tab into the canvas lets the user arrow-key through nodes (the canvas captures keyboard events and triggers the same focus mechanic). Reduced-motion: focus camera moves are instant (no lerp), pulse animation is removed (replaced with a small static '!' badge), and orbit is disabled (camera locks). Color: status is supplemented by an icon shape (circle = healthy, triangle = warn, square = error) on close-LOD nodes.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) 3-tier LOD system (billboard → 3D box → HTML overlay) lets 800 nodes coexist in a single scene without performance degradation. 2) Focus interaction dims non-neighbors to 40% — the 'pinned attention' pattern that makes complex graphs scannable during incidents. 3) Hierarchical tier-based layout (Y axis encodes tier) instead of a chaotic ball — 3D earns its weight by eliminating edge crossings.

title: Calendar event creation with gestural drag-time-slot on mobile and click-drag-paint on desktop
design_brief: You're building the event creation flow for a calendar app (think Cron + Notion Calendar + Fantastical + Google Calendar). Pain point: most calendar apps ship the same modal-based 'Create Event' flow on every device, ignoring how differently people use a calendar with a thumb vs a mouse. The bar: Cron/Notion Calendar (the desktop gold standard), Fantastical's mobile gestures, Things 3's quick entry.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Floating + button that opens a modal with 6 fields on mobile
Same modal on desktop — wastes the desktop's spatial advantage
Desktop click-drag to create event that requires a 'Save' modal after
Mobile time pickers that are the iOS wheel everywhere — even on Android
'All-day' toggle as a separate field instead of a gesture
Generic 12pt time slots on mobile that are smaller than a fingertip
ux_reasoning:
Calendar creation needs to feel like writing on paper. On desktop, that means click-drag to paint a time range, type immediately to add a title. On mobile, that means long-press a slot to claim it, then a bottom-sheet quick-entry where the time is already set.

Desktop interaction: hover a time slot, the slot subtly highlights. Click-drag down to paint a range. On mouse-up, the range becomes a real event with an inline editable title — focus jumps directly to the title input, no modal. Cmd+Enter saves; Esc cancels. The event surfaces with a colored bar in the user's default calendar color. Drag the bottom edge to extend; drag the middle to move; drag the top to change start time. This is Cron's exact interaction.

Mobile interaction: tap-and-hold a time slot for 200ms to claim it (haptic confirms). A bottom sheet rises (40% height) with a pre-filled time range and a focused title input. The sheet has a 'grabber' at the top — drag up to expand to full sheet (revealing more fields: location, attendees, calendar), drag down to dismiss. The time can be adjusted by dragging the event bar within the visible calendar UNDER the sheet — the calendar is still alive and editable while the sheet is open. This is the killer move: the sheet doesn't take over; it cohabitates.

Quick-add input on both: a single line that accepts natural language ('lunch with sarah tomorrow 12-1pm at gjelina'). On desktop, this lives in the top-right and is summoned with Cmd+N. On mobile, it's hidden behind a tap on the date header. Both feed the same parser.

Month view differs sharply by device. Desktop: a 7-column grid with mini-events as colored bars. Mobile: a single column showing 14 days vertically (like Apple Calendar's iPad mode adapted for phones), with a dot for each event — full month gridded view shouldn't exist on a 6" screen.

Rejected: pinch-to-zoom on mobile timeline. Tested it — users zoomed accidentally, lost context. Fixed time scale + scroll is better. Also rejected: a unified 'Create' button that opens different UI per device. Visible affordances should match the modality, not the brand.

design_tokens.colors: Time slots: alternating oklch(0.99 0.005 250) and oklch(0.98 0.005 250) for hour bands. Event bar: user calendar color at 80% opacity with 1px solid border in 100% opacity. Selection paint (during drag): user color at 30% opacity dashed border. Bottom sheet bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250) light / oklch(0.18 0.005 250) dark.
design_tokens.typography: Event title: Inter 13px medium (desktop), 15px medium (mobile). Time labels: 11px tabular-nums (desktop), 12px (mobile). Bottom sheet input: 17px (avoids iOS zoom-on-focus, looks proper-sized).
design_tokens.spacing: Desktop time slot: 60px / hour (4 quarter-hour subdivisions of 15px each). Mobile time slot: 80px / hour (still 4 subdivisions, larger touch). Event minimum: 30min on desktop, 45min on mobile (touch ergonomics).
design_tokens.motion: Desktop drag-paint: real-time, no animation. Event resolve on mouse-up: 80ms opacity transition to full color. Mobile sheet rise: 350ms cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) — the iOS sheet curve. Sheet drag: 1:1 finger tracking, snap-back on release if past threshold.

component_architecture: <Calendar /> root with <DayView /> children. <DayView /> uses an interaction adapter: DesktopAdapter handles mouse drag → paint → inline edit, TouchAdapter handles long-press → bottom sheet. Both feed into the same useCreateEvent() hook which holds the pending event state. <BottomSheet /> is a reusable primitive with drag affordance. <QuickAdd /> is the natural-language input shared across modalities.
interaction_details: Desktop: click-drag down on time grid paints a range. Mouse-up locks the range; cursor jumps to inline title input. Type title. Cmd+Enter saves; Esc discards.
Desktop event resize: drag bottom edge to change end; drag middle to move; drag top to change start. All 1:1 with cursor, no snap unless Shift held.
Mobile: long-press 200ms on a time slot — single haptic tap confirms. Bottom sheet rises with title input focused and time pre-filled.
Mobile sheet: drag grabber up to expand (more fields), drag down past 120px to dismiss. Calendar UNDER the sheet remains touchable — drag event bar to adjust time without dismissing the sheet.
Quick-add: Cmd+N on desktop opens top-right NLP input; tap date header on mobile opens it. Same parser ('lunch tomorrow 12-1pm at gjelina').
accessibility_considerations: Both modes use proper <dialog>-like semantics for the title-edit moment. Bottom sheet is role='dialog' aria-modal='true' on mobile with proper focus trap. Desktop inline edit doesn't need a dialog — it's just an inline input with focus. Keyboard alternatives on mobile: a tap on the '+' icon in the day header is equivalent to long-press. Quick-add NLP input announces parsed result via aria-live ('Parsed: lunch on Friday from 12 to 1 PM at Gjelina'). Reduced-motion: sheet animation becomes instant snap; event drag-paint has no animation regardless. Color: event status (tentative, confirmed) is supplemented by a small icon, not just color.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Mobile bottom sheet leaves the calendar live and touchable underneath — adjust time by dragging the event itself without dismissing the sheet. 2) Desktop click-drag-paint with focus-to-title on release means an event can be created and named in 3 seconds without ever opening a modal. 3) Same natural-language quick-add parser feeds both modalities — 'lunch tomorrow 12-1pm' just works on either device.

title: Focus management in dynamic SPA with route changes, async content, and modal stacks
design_brief: You're building the navigation + content shell for a multi-route SPA (think Linear's app shell, GitHub's PR navigation, Notion's page transitions). Pain point: most SPAs handle focus terribly — route changes leave focus on the link that triggered nav, async content loads silently, and modal close returns focus to nowhere. Screen reader users get hopelessly lost. The bar: GitHub's accessibility work, Adobe Spectrum's focus management primitives, Web.dev's focus-visible guidance.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Letting focus stay on the nav link after route change (next Tab takes user to a random place)
Loading skeletons with no announcement, just visual
Modal close that returns focus to document.body
autoFocus on every input — fights with intentional focus order
Focus traps that don't release on Esc
Hiding focus rings globally with :focus { outline: none }
ux_reasoning:
Focus is the screen reader user's cursor. Every dynamic UI change has a focus implication; ignoring it is the dominant accessibility failure in SPAs. The solution is a small but rigorous set of patterns enforced by primitives.

Route change: on navigation, focus moves to the new page's <h1> (set tabIndex={-1} on the h1 so it's programmatically focusable but not in the natural tab order). This is the GOV.UK pattern, tested with thousands of users including screen reader users — it announces 'Settings, heading level 1' on arrival, which is the perfect orientation. Visual focus ring on the h1 is suppressed (it would look weird), but the SR cursor lands there.

Async content: when a section's content is being loaded, the skeleton has aria-busy='true' on its container. Screen readers know to wait for the content. When the content loads, focus does NOT move (would be jarring) — but an aria-live='polite' region announces 'Content loaded'. The skeleton-to-content swap is structural; the announcement is the signal.

Modal stacks: each modal pushes its previous focus owner onto a stack. On open, focus moves to the modal's primary action (often the input, sometimes the close button). On close, focus pops back to the previous owner. Focus trap is implemented via a useFocusTrap() hook that captures Tab/Shift+Tab at the modal boundary. Esc always closes the topmost modal and restores its previous focus.

Nested modals (modal opens modal — rare but real): each level pushes onto the focus stack independently. Closing in order pops in order. We disallow more than 3 levels deep — that's a sign of a confused UI, not an accessibility feature.

Focus visible vs focus: we use :focus-visible globally. Mouse clicks don't show a focus ring; keyboard focus does. This is the right default — sighted-mouse users don't need the visual marker, keyboard users always do.

Live region strategy: ONE aria-live='polite' region for app-wide announcements (route changes, async loads, save confirmations). ONE aria-live='assertive' region for errors and warnings only. Sprinkling aria-live everywhere creates announcement collisions. Centralizing them gives us coordinated, debounced narration.

Rejected: jumping focus to 'main' on route change. Sounds reasonable but 'main' is announced as 'main landmark' which is meaningless; the page's h1 is the orientation cue. Also rejected: a 'Skip to content' link. Adds chrome for one user type at the cost of every user; route-change focus to h1 obviates the need.

design_tokens.colors: Focus ring: 2px solid oklch(0.55 0.18 250) at 2px offset. NEVER box-shadow (forced-colors strips). Background dim under modal: oklch(0.18 0.005 250 / 0.6).
design_tokens.typography: Page h1: Inter 24px medium with font-feature-settings: 'kern', 'liga'. Modal title: 17px medium. Skeleton: matches real content type ramp (not generic shimmer).
design_tokens.spacing: Focus offset: 2px. Modal padding: 24px. Modal width: max(360px, 30vw).
design_tokens.motion: Focus changes: no animation (focus is structural, not decorative). Modal open: 150ms ease-out opacity. Modal close: 100ms ease-in opacity. Reduced-motion: same — already minimal.

component_architecture: <RouteContainer /> renders the current route's component and sets focus to its h1 on mount via useRouteFocus(). <Modal /> uses useFocusTrap() + useFocusStack() (a context that maintains the stack across nested modals). <AsyncSection isLoading={...} /> wraps its content with aria-busy. <Announcer /> is the single source of polite live updates, used via useAnnounce(message).
interaction_details: Route change: focus jumps to the new page's h1 (with tabIndex=-1 so it's silently programmatically focusable); screen reader announces 'Settings, heading level 1'.
Async section loading: container has aria-busy='true'; on load, aria-live announces 'Settings loaded' without moving focus.
Modal open: previous focus owner pushed to stack; focus moves to modal's first focusable; announcement 'Edit profile dialog opened'.
Modal close (Esc or click outside): focus pops from stack, returns to the element that opened the modal — never document.body.
Nested modal: stack supports up to 3 levels. Close-in-reverse pops correctly. Beyond 3 levels: dev warning + UI design red flag.
accessibility_considerations: This entire task IS the accessibility considerations. Specifics: :focus-visible globally (no outline on mouse, always on keyboard), single centralized polite + assertive live regions (no announcement collision), focus stack on modals (no orphaned focus), route-change focus to h1 (orientation via heading), async aria-busy + load announcement (signal without disorientation). Reduced-motion: modal opacity transitions are already 150ms — no kinetics to disable. Forced-colors mode: focus ring is 2px outline (CanvasText in forced-colors).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Route change moves focus to the new page's h1 with tabIndex=-1 — the GOV.UK orientation pattern that announces the page identity perfectly. 2) Single centralized polite + assertive live regions with debouncing prevents announcement collisions during rapid state changes. 3) Focus stack on modals ensures close ALWAYS returns to the opener — even through nested modal sequences.

title: Image gallery with LQIP, content-aware loading priority, and shape-preserving placeholders
design_brief: You're building the image gallery for a photography portfolio site (think Unsplash + Pinterest + a hosted-photographer Squarespace site). Pain point: most galleries either dump 50 full-resolution images causing 10MB+ initial loads, or use generic gray placeholders that pop into images with a 200ms fade — both feel slow. The bar: Medium's image loading (the original LQIP pattern), Unsplash's progressive enhancement, Vercel's Next/Image at its best.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Generic 1:1 gray placeholders that don't match actual image aspect ratio
Blur-up that takes 400ms and is more distracting than helpful
Loading 'spinner' overlaid on placeholder
Lazy-load triggered only when image enters viewport (too late — scroll already there)
Skeleton with 'shimmer' animation for static content
Single resolution for all viewport widths
ux_reasoning:
Perceived performance for images is about ALWAYS having SOMETHING in the slot that hints at what's coming. Three layers:

First layer (instant): a CSS aspect-ratio box renders before the image starts loading. This prevents layout shift — the slot exists at correct dimensions and the page reflow happens once, on initial render. Critical: we get aspect ratio from a metadata index (each image record stores width + height), not from waiting on image load.

Second layer (sub-100ms): a Low-Quality Image Placeholder (LQIP) — a 24×24 webp encoded as base64 (~600 bytes) inlined directly into the HTML. This is the Medium pattern, refined. We pre-generate at build time, NOT request-time. When the slot renders, the LQIP is already there as a CSS background-image — no network request. It's heavily blurred so the low resolution doesn't show; the eye sees 'something photographic, with these colors, in this composition'.

Third layer (the actual image): we pick the responsive srcset based on the slot's actual width (via the sizes attribute), then load it. Modern browsers handle this perfectly with srcset+sizes. On load, the full image fades in over 100ms (NOT 300+ — long fades make slow networks feel slower because users see the placeholder for longer).

Loading priority: visible-in-viewport-on-mount images load with fetchpriority='high'. Images within 1.5 viewports below: loading='lazy' with fetchpriority='low'. Images further than 1.5 viewports: defer until IntersectionObserver fires at 800px ahead of viewport (so they load BEFORE entering, not WHEN entering — the user never sees the gradient transition).

The shape-preserving placeholder is the key UX detail. Many sites use a single aspect ratio container that doesn't match real images, causing them to stretch or letterbox on load. We use the metadata's exact aspect ratio per image. Slot looks correct from before the LQIP loads.

For a masonry/grid layout, this matters double — the layout is computed from aspect ratios. If the placeholder is wrong, the layout reflows on every image load (the worst possible UX). With shape-preserving placeholders, the layout is correct from render.

Rejected: a dominant-color placeholder (a solid colored rectangle matching the image's average color). Tested it; works for some images, looks weird for others. LQIP is strictly better at the same byte cost. Also rejected: progressive JPEG. Most browsers don't render progressive JPEGs progressively anymore, and webp + LQIP is smaller anyway.

design_tokens.colors: Placeholder bg before LQIP: oklch(0.95 0.005 250) — neutral but warm. LQIP blur: 24px (via CSS filter). No 'shimmer' colors — shimmer is for skeleton text, not images.
design_tokens.typography: Image caption: Inter 13px regular. Loading indicator (if any): not used — placeholders are the signal.
design_tokens.spacing: Gallery grid: 8px gap. Image rounded corners: 4px (subtle — photography deserves restraint).
design_tokens.motion: Image fade-in on load: 100ms ease-out opacity from 0 → 1. NO blur transition (would compound with LQIP blur). NO scale animation.

component_architecture: > generic. Each image has { src, srcset, sizes, lqipBase64, width, height, alt }. renders the aspect-ratio container, sets LQIP as background, and renders the with srcset/sizes when ready to load. uses IntersectionObserver with rootMargin: '800px' to trigger load before visibility. Loading priority based on initial render position.
interaction_details: Initial render: every slot has correct aspect ratio AND a 24x24 LQIP showing — the layout is final from the first paint.
Visible images: load with fetchpriority='high'. Slots within 1.5 viewports below: lazy + low priority. Further: deferred until IntersectionObserver fires at 800px ahead.
On image load: full image fades in over 100ms — no blur transition, no scale, no spinner. The LQIP was the loading state.
No layout shift on any image load — the aspect-ratio container holds the space from initial render.
User scrolling fast: deferred images load 800px ahead of viewport, so they're ready before visible — the user never sees the placeholder for in-flow images.
accessibility_considerations: Each image has a proper alt attribute from the metadata. Decorative LQIP background is aria-hidden. Gallery uses semantic <ul><li> for list semantics. Captions (if present) use <figcaption>. Reduced-motion: the 100ms opacity fade is already minimal; no change needed. Loading state is non-blocking — screen readers don't get spammed with 'loading...' because there's nothing to announce; the alt text is read when the image becomes meaningful (loaded or not).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Shape-preserving placeholders use per-image aspect ratios from metadata — layout is final from the first paint, never reflows on image load. 2) Inline 600-byte LQIP base64 as CSS background means the placeholder is present BEFORE any network request — zero perceived latency to first impression. 3) Three-tier loading priority (high/low/defer) with 800px IntersectionObserver margin means in-flow images load BEFORE entering viewport, never WHEN entering.

title: Brutalist directory listing with system-font typography and unapologetic grid
design_brief: You're building the public directory/index page for an artist-run online publication or a curated catalog (think Are.na's directory pages, Daniel Eckler's lists, Cabinet Magazine archive, Bloomberg Businessweek's data sections). Pain point: most directory listings adopt a 'card grid with avatars + tags' formula. The bar: Are.na's deliberate visual restraint, OFFSCREEN Magazine's typography, Jen Liu's portfolios, Karpf.studio.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Card grid with shadowed white tiles
Tag chips in pastel colors
Web-safe sans-serif (Helvetica) used at all sizes without consideration
Centered alignment everywhere
Hover lifts that simulate physical depth
Animated underlines on hover
Generic icons next to every text field
ux_reasoning:
Brutalism in web design is widely misunderstood as 'ugly on purpose'. Real brutalism (architectural and digital) is about: structural honesty, system fonts, unapologetic grids, refusal of ornament, and rhythm through density. Cabinet Magazine's website is the canonical example.

Layout: a 12-column grid with NO container max-width. The content spans edge-to-edge. The brutalist move is to TRUST the screen, not coddle it with safe 1280px frames. Vertical rhythm is enforced by a 24px baseline grid; every element snaps to multiples of 24.

Typography: system fonts ONLY. -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif for sans. Times New Roman, Georgia, serif for serif. No web fonts. This isn't laziness; it's a commitment to ZERO render-blocking font loading and the texture of each user's OS. The directory looks slightly different on each platform — that's a feature.

The directory itself is a long vertical list. Each entry is a single row spanning 12 columns, with columns assigned to specific data: cols 1-2 for index number (tabular, large), cols 3-7 for title, cols 8-9 for author, cols 10-11 for date, col 12 for category abbreviation. Row height is 96px. Hovering a row inverts the colors (full black bg, white text) instantly — no transition. This is the brutalist 'interaction state': declarative, immediate, no kinetic ornament.

Number style: index numbers are 64px Times New Roman, italic, in a faded gray. They give the page rhythm AND signal 'this is a catalog, not a feed'. The number is the most editorial element; it sets the register.

Text hierarchy: titles are 18px medium sans, author 13px regular, date 11px tabular-nums. No uppercase for stylistic effect (cliché). Old-style figures in the year (1997 looks better in oldstyle than tabular). Underlines on links are 1px solid (not the default browser thick double-line), positioned via text-underline-offset: 0.2em.

Grid lines: 0.5px lines between rows. Not 1px — 0.5px is the brutalist tell, the awareness that 1px on a retina screen is actually 2px. Hairlines force the viewer to look closer.

Filters: at the top, filter chips are ALL-CAPS labels in 11px tracking-wider, separated by 1px vertical lines. Active filters are inverted (black bg). No animations. No 'X' to remove — click again to toggle.

Rejected: a 'mode toggle' between brutalist and 'comfortable' versions. Aesthetic conviction can't be a settings menu. Brutalism is the whole point. Also rejected: dark mode that's a brand-color dark. We respect prefers-color-scheme but the dark version is just white-on-black with the same restraint — the aesthetic doesn't need branding.

design_tokens.colors: Single pair: oklch(0.12 0.005 250) (near-black, slight cool warmth) on oklch(0.99 0.005 80) (paper-warm white). NO accent colors. NO greens, blues, oranges. The only accent is the inversion on hover.
design_tokens.typography: Sans: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, sans-serif. Serif: Times New Roman, Times, Georgia, serif. Sizes: 64px serif italic for index, 18px sans medium for title, 13px sans regular for author, 11px sans tabular-nums for date and category. Line-height: 1.0 for index (tight), 1.4 for titles, 1.5 for body.
design_tokens.spacing: 24px baseline grid. Row height: 96px (4 baseline units). Column gap: 16px. No content padding on container — content meets the viewport edge.
design_tokens.motion: None. Inversion on hover is instant (no transition). The only motion in the entire interface.

component_architecture: <Directory<TEntry extends DirectoryEntry>> generic. <Directory /> renders a header + filter bar + a list of <DirectoryRow />. Filter state is local with no animation. The component is intentionally simple — brutalism rewards architectural simplicity. Tokens are defined as CSS custom properties; component reads them via Tailwind arbitrary classes for clarity.
interaction_details: Hover any row: instant inversion (no transition) — full black bg, white text. The 64px serif number jumps from 30% to 60% opacity.
Click a filter chip: chip inverts (black bg / white text); list filters to that category. Multiple filters apply with OR logic. Click again to toggle off.
No animations, no transitions, no scroll-triggered reveals. The page IS, period.
Edge-to-edge layout — no max-width container. Trust the user's screen.
Tabular-nums for IDs and dates, oldstyle for the index numbers. Typography rhythm is the entire design system.
accessibility_considerations: Semantic HTML throughout: <ol> for ordered list, real <a> for links, <button aria-pressed> for filter toggles. Inversion-on-hover is also inversion-on-focus (so keyboard users get the same affordance). Color contrast: ink on paper is 14:1 (AAA). Reduced-motion: there's no motion to reduce. Forced-colors: the page renders correctly because we use semantic colors (not box-shadow effects). System fonts mean zero font-loading delay and the page is keyboard-navigable from first paint.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Edge-to-edge layout with 0.5px hairline rules — the brutalist refusal of safe-zone containers and the awareness that retina pixels demand thinner strokes. 2) 64px italic serif index numbers in oldstyle figures next to 11px tabular-nums dates — typographic rhythm doing the entire job of visual hierarchy without ornament. 3) Instant inversion-on-hover (no transition) as the only interaction state — brutalism is declarative, not kinetic.

title: Dashboard tile system with content-aware layout reflow and density toggle
design_brief: You're building the homepage dashboard for a DevOps platform (think Vercel + Posthog + Render + Datadog). Engineers want at-a-glance status across 8-30 projects/services. Pain point: most dashboards either fixed-grid every card (wasting space when there are 3 projects, overflowing when there are 30) or let users hand-arrange tiles (configuration UI nobody uses). The bar: Vercel's project list, Datadog's host map, Render's services view.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hand-drag tile arrangement with grid snap
Fixed 3-column grid that becomes a single column on mobile
Cards with 200px of padding for 60px of content
Status circle + percentage + sparkline + 'View details' button on every tile (overkill)
Empty 'You have no projects' state with a 200px illustration
Density toggle that's hidden in settings
ux_reasoning:
A dashboard tile system is a constraint solver: given N items of varying importance, fit them in a viewport such that important items are large enough to scan, recent items are most accessible, and nothing wastes space.

Layout: content-aware tile sizing. Each project has an inferred 'weight' (recent activity, alert count, user-pinned status). Heavy tiles take 2x2 cells; medium take 2x1 (wide); light take 1x1 (square). A bin-packing algorithm (greedy, computed once on load + on resize) fills the grid. This is the Pinterest layout transposed for tiles of variable importance, not just variable image height.

Density toggle: a single button in the top-right cycles 3 states: 'comfortable' (current default, generous spacing, weight-based sizing), 'compact' (50% tile size, all tiles uniform 1x1), 'list' (vertical list with full row per project, like a list view in Mail). The same data, three densities — power users compress, casual users breathe.

Tile content: status indicator (1px colored stripe top edge — same trick as the log viewer), project name, ONE meaningful metric (deploys today, requests/min, error count), and a 32px sparkline. Heavy tiles add a list of recent deploys or alerts. Tiles don't have CTAs ('View details' buttons) — clicking anywhere on the tile opens details.

Reflow on resize: when the viewport changes, we re-run the bin packing with a FLIP animation choreographing tile movements over 350ms. This is the only motion in the dashboard, and it's information-bearing (you can SEE how density shifted, so you don't feel lost).

Empty state: a single tile placeholder 'Connect your first project →' that opens the new-project flow. No illustration, no sad face.

Performance: every tile's sparkline data is loaded on-demand via Suspense — initial render uses status + metric (which fits in the project metadata response), sparkline data hydrates progressively. Tiles below the fold (rare in a tile grid) are virtualized.

Rejected: customizable tile sizes by user. No one configures their dashboard; we infer from signal. Also rejected: a separate 'pinned' section. Pinning becomes a per-user state that fights with the algorithmic ordering; instead, user-favorited projects boost their inferred weight.

design_tokens.colors: Status stripe: green oklch(0.6 0.14 145), yellow oklch(0.75 0.16 80), red oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Tile bg: oklch(0.99 0.005 250) light / oklch(0.18 0.005 250) dark. Tile border: 0.5px solid oklch(0.92 0.005 250) — hairline. Hover: bg shifts to oklch(0.97 0.005 250). NO gradients.
design_tokens.typography: Tile title: Inter 14px medium. Metric label: 11px uppercase tracking-wider oklch(0.55). Metric value: Inter 22px tabular-nums (medium tile), 16px (compact). Sparkline: no text labels, just shape.
design_tokens.spacing: Cell size: 240px (1x1 tile). Gap: 12px. Tile padding: 16px (comfortable), 8px (compact). List mode: 56px row height.
design_tokens.motion: Layout reflow on density change or resize: 350ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) per tile via FLIP. Tile hover: 80ms ease-out bg transition. Sparkline draws once on mount, no animation.

component_architecture: <Dashboard<TProject extends Project> projects={[]}>. Tiles auto-size via <Tile size='1x1' | '2x1' | '2x2' />. Bin packing happens in a useDashboardLayout(projects, viewportCols) hook returning a [{ project, size, x, y }] array. <DensityToggle /> controls a context. <SparklineCell /> uses Suspense for lazy data load. FLIP reflow via a useFlipLayout() hook that captures previous bounds and applies inverse transforms.
interaction_details: Density toggle: cycle through comfortable / compact / list. Layout reflows with 350ms FLIP — tiles smoothly resize and reposition.
Tile click: open project (no separate 'View details' button — the tile IS the link).
Heavy projects (weight > 0.66) get 2x2 tiles in comfortable mode, communicating importance via size.
List mode: same data as full-width rows, useful for >50 projects or for keyboard-power-users who j/k through.
Resize browser: tiles re-pack with FLIP. The user sees their projects flow into new arrangement, never disappear or jump.
accessibility_considerations: Density toggle uses role='radiogroup' with role='radio' buttons — correct ARIA pattern.
