Each tile is a real button with aria-label including project name + status + metric. Status stripe color is supplemented by the status word in aria-label. List mode preserves the same data + actions. Reduced-motion: FLIP reflow becomes instant. Color: status stripe colors meet 3:1 against tile bg; metric value text meets AAA. Focus ring on tiles is a 2px outline (not a shadow) for forced-colors compatibility.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Content-aware bin-packing assigns tile sizes by inferred project weight — important projects naturally take more space, no manual config. 2) Three-state density toggle gives one dashboard three personalities — same data, different needs. 3) FLIP-choreographed reflow on density change lets users watch their projects flow into the new arrangement — no jarring jump.

title: Spreadsheet cell editing with type-ahead, range selection, and formula autocomplete
design_brief: You're building the cell editing experience for a collaborative spreadsheet (think Google Sheets + Airtable + Notion's database + Equals). Pain point: most web-spreadsheet clones either fight with browser keyboard defaults or oversimplify (you can't select a range with Shift+arrows in many of them). The bar: Google Sheets (the gold standard everyone copies poorly), Notion's database keyboard model, Equals' formula UX.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Double-click required to enter edit mode (slow)
Esc to leave edit mode but keeping changes (confusing)
Selection that breaks when scrolling
Formula autocomplete that's a dropdown 200px tall blocking the cell
Generic 'Save' button after edits — should commit on Enter/blur
Selection that only works with mouse, not Shift+click
ux_reasoning:
Spreadsheet keyboard handling is the most stress-tested interaction model in computing — Lotus 1-2-3 set conventions in 1983 that still apply. Breaking them is hubris.

Four states per cell: NORMAL (cell focused but not editing), EDIT (typing replaces or appends to cell content), FORMULA (cell content starts with =, autocomplete active), SELECTION-EXTEND (Shift held + arrows extend a range from anchor).

State transitions: type any printable character in NORMAL → enters EDIT and the character becomes cell content (replacing). Enter in NORMAL → EDIT with cursor at end (preserving content). F2 → same. Esc in EDIT → cancel, revert to NORMAL. Enter in EDIT → commit + move down. Tab in EDIT → commit + move right. Arrow keys in EDIT → commit + move in arrow direction (Sheets convention; some users prefer arrow-inside-cell — debatable, but the productivity model favors quick navigation).

Formula mode: typing = enters EDIT with formula autocomplete attached. The autocomplete shows function names with their signatures (SUM(range), VLOOKUP(value, range, index, [exact])). Selecting a function name inserts it with cursor at first argument. Clicking other cells while in FORMULA mode adds them as references (the killer feature). Tab cycles through suggestions; Enter accepts.

Range selection: Shift+arrow extends range from the anchor cell. Cmd+Shift+arrow extends to data edge (last non-empty cell in that direction). Click anchor + Shift+click far cell = rectangular range. Cmd+click adds discontiguous cells to a multi-range selection — this is the underused power feature.

Visualization: focused cell has a 2px outline in user-color (collab-aware), selected range has a faint user-color overlay. NEVER use brand color — collaborative context demands per-user color. Multi-range selection: each range is outlined separately with a faint dashed border showing it's a discontiguous group.

Motion: zero. Spreadsheet users hate animations. Cursor moves are instant. Range fills are instant.

Rejected: a 'Save' indicator that flashes after each commit. Sheets has this and it's mostly noise. We rely on optimistic UI; failures show inline as red cell borders. Also rejected: a separate 'formula bar' at the top. Sheets has this; Notion doesn't. We don't either — the cell IS the formula bar, with the autocomplete attached below for context.

design_tokens.colors: Focused cell outline: 2px user-color. Selected range overlay: user-color at 10% alpha. Multi-range dashed border: 1px dashed user-color. Cell error border: 2px oklch(0.6 0.18 25). Formula autocomplete bg: oklch(0.18 0.005 250) for the popover (dark for legibility against light spreadsheet).
design_tokens.typography: Cell content: SF Mono / JetBrains Mono 12px (numbers and formulas) or Inter 13px (text). Autocomplete function names: 12px monospace with arg names in italic 11px.
design_tokens.spacing: Cell default: 96px wide × 24px tall (Sheets-default proportions). Autocomplete popover: 240px wide, anchored below cell.
design_tokens.motion: None on selection or editing. The only transition: autocomplete fade-in over 80ms ease-out.

component_architecture: <Spreadsheet<TCell extends CellValue>> generic. <Cell /> reads its state from SpreadsheetStateContext. Keyboard handling lives at the spreadsheet root (single listener — never per-cell). State: focused coords, edit buffer, selection ranges, formula autocomplete state. Formula parser is a small recursive-descent parser for cell refs + functions, ~200 LOC.
interaction_details: NORMAL mode: arrow keys move focus, any printable char enters EDIT with that char as initial content, Enter or F2 enters EDIT preserving existing content.
EDIT mode: type to modify, Enter commits + moves down, Tab commits + moves right, Esc cancels and reverts, arrow keys commit + move in direction.
FORMULA mode: starting buffer with = activates autocomplete popover; Tab cycles suggestions; Enter accepts. Clicking other cells while in formula mode inserts their refs.
Shift+arrow: extends rectangular selection from anchor. Cmd+Shift+arrow: extends to data edge.
Cmd+click cells while a range is selected: adds discontiguous range to multi-selection (dashed borders communicate the group).
accessibility_considerations: role='grid' on root, role='row' on rows, role='gridcell' on cells with aria-selected reflecting focus. Single tab stop into the grid (the standard WAI-ARIA grid pattern). Keyboard model is the entire interaction — no mouse-only paths. Formula autocomplete uses aria-haspopup and aria-activedescendant for screen reader compatibility. Reduced-motion: autocomplete fade-in is 80ms; could be removed but it's already imperceptible. Color: focused cell outline color is supplemented by visible focus ring; range selection has both bg overlay and outline border so colorblind users can identify it. Cell content is always perceivable regardless of selection state.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) State-machine model with 4 distinct modes (NORMAL / EDIT / FORMULA / EXTEND) gives the user a coherent mental model — every key has a single meaning per mode. 2) Click-cells-while-in-formula auto-inserts cell refs — the spreadsheet's most-loved feature, faithfully implemented. 3) Multi-range Cmd+click selection with dashed-border grouping — discontiguous selection is the underused power feature most clones omit.

title: Selection presence in long lists with avatar pinning and viewport telegraphy
design_brief: You're building selection presence for a collaborative kanban board (think Linear's cycles + Trello's collab + Height's projects). Multiple team members are looking at the same list of 200 issues; you need to show who's looking at what. Pain point: most collab tools show avatars only when users hover/click — passive presence is invisible. The bar: Figma's selection awareness, Linear's collab indicators on issue rows, Pitch's slide thumbnails with collaborator pins.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Avatar stack in the header showing 'who's in this list' without context of WHAT they're looking at
Per-row avatar dot ONLY when a user has the row selected (silent on view)
Modal 'X is editing this item' notifications
Cursors for every user superimposed on a 200-row list (chaos)
Same color for selection and presence
Auto-scroll to where another user is — hostile to local navigation
ux_reasoning:
In a long list, presence has two distinct signals: who is VIEWING (their viewport contains the row) and who has the row SELECTED (focused, possibly about to edit). Conflating these is the original sin.

VIEWING signal: when User A's viewport contains rows 12-28, those rows get a 2px-wide colored stripe on the right edge in A's color. The stripe is faint (40% alpha) but visible. It tells you 'A is currently looking at this row'. As A scrolls, the stripes follow their viewport (debounced 300ms to avoid flicker during fast scroll). This is the viewport telegraphy — at-a-glance awareness without avatar clutter.

SELECTION signal: when A clicks a row to focus it (vs just scroll past), an avatar circle (20px) anchors to the row's right edge with A's name visible on hover. The selection ring on the row is also in A's color (lighter). Two distinct signals, two distinct visual treatments.

Avatar pinning: in long lists, the user might want to know where their teammates are without scrolling. A right-side rail (32px wide) shows tiny avatar dots positioned by their selection-row's vertical position relative to the visible list. Click an avatar dot to scroll to where they are. This is the killer pattern — the rail is essentially a multi-cursor minimap.

Idle handling: if a user is on the list but hasn't moved their viewport in 60s, their viewport stripes fade to 20% opacity. After 5 minutes, the stripes are gone but the avatar in the right rail remains, dimmed. Presence has gradation.

User-color discipline: each user has ONE color in the workspace, used consistently across the entire product (cursors here, presence in docs, comment threads). Color is identity. Random assignment per session is hostile.

Motion: stripes appear/disappear instantly. Avatar dots in the rail move smoothly to follow user selections (200ms ease-out — they're a minimap, smoothness is information).

Rejected: showing read receipts ('X viewed this row 2m ago'). Tested it; users found it surveillance-y, not collaborative. Live viewing is OK; historical viewing is creepy. Also rejected: hover-to-reveal presence. Passive presence must be visible without interaction; otherwise it's not presence.

design_tokens.colors: User colors from the 12-color palette (deterministic per user across product). Viewport stripe: user color at 40% alpha. Selection ring on row: user color at 60% alpha, 2px. Avatar dot: solid user color, 20px circle. Rail bg: oklch(0.97 0.005 250) light / oklch(0.20 0.005 250) dark.
design_tokens.typography: Avatar tooltip: Inter 11px medium. Row content: same as elsewhere in the app.
design_tokens.spacing: Viewport stripe: 2px wide, full row height, anchored right. Avatar circle: 20px. Right rail: 32px wide, fixed.
design_tokens.motion: Viewport stripe appear/disappear: instant. Avatar dot in rail movement: 200ms ease-out translateY. Idle fade (60s no movement): 1000ms linear opacity to 0.2.

component_architecture: <CollaborativePresenceLayer<TItem> /> wraps the list. <ListWithPresence /> renders each row with conditional stripe and selection ring based on presence map. <PresenceRail /> renders avatar dots positioned by user's selected-row index. PresenceContext provides per-user { viewportRange: [startIdx, endIdx], selectedItemIdx, lastMovedAt }. Updates come from websocket subscriptions; each user broadcasts their viewport indices on scroll (debounced 200ms).
interaction_details: VIEWING: a thin colored stripe on row's right edge in each viewer's color (40% alpha). Stripes follow viewport as users scroll (debounced 200ms).
SELECTION: a 2px ring around row in user's color (60% alpha), plus a 20px avatar circle at row's right edge. Multiple selectors stack with -space overlap.
Right rail: 32px wide column showing all users' avatar dots at their selected-row's vertical position. Click avatar to scroll-jump to that user's row.
Idle (60s): viewport stripes fade to 20% alpha. After 5min: removed; only rail avatar remains, dimmed.
Same row selected by multiple users: each user's ring color is layered (slight color blend on row outline); avatars stack to show all selectors.
accessibility_considerations: Presence rail is a real <aside> with aria-label and each avatar dot is a <button> with aria-label='Jump to Alice'. Viewport stripes are aria-hidden (decorative for sighted users; presence info is conveyed via the rail for SR users). Selection-row aria-label includes 'also selected by Alice, Bob' so SR users know about collaborator presence on the focused row. Reduced-motion: rail avatar movement becomes instant snap. Color: user colors are supplemented by name tooltips and aria-labels — never color-only signaling. Keyboard navigation: Tab moves between rail avatars; Enter jumps.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Two distinct presence signals (viewport stripe for VIEWING, ring + avatar for SELECTION) — passive awareness without conflating attention with action. 2) Right rail of avatar dots acts as a multi-user minimap — click to jump to any collaborator's selection. 3) Idle gradation (60s viewport fade, 5min stripe removal, rail persistence) — presence has lifespan, the list self-cleans.

title: Settings toggle with morphing icon and persistent change confirmation
design_brief: You're building the settings page toggles for a user-account UI (think Apple Settings, Linear's preferences, Notion's settings). Pain point: most toggles are visual switches — slide left, slide right, color changes. They don't communicate WHAT changed semantically (push notifications enabled vs disabled looks visually identical except for direction), and they don't acknowledge changes for irreversible/important settings. The bar: Apple's Settings toggles, Linear's preference panel, Things 3's settings.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Generic Tailwind switch with on/off colors
Confirmation modal after every toggle ('Are you sure?')
Toast notification that the setting changed (the toggle's state is the confirmation)
Spring-bounce animation on the toggle handle
Icon that's identical in both states
Disabled state for irreversible toggles (annoying — better to confirm in place)
ux_reasoning:
A toggle is the smallest UI element with the biggest semantic weight: it changes user-state, often persistently. Each toggle should communicate three things — its current state, what it does, and (for important changes) acknowledgment that the change was saved.

The morphing icon: instead of just a slider with a colored bg, the toggle has an iconographic representation that MORPHS between states. For a 'Sound effects' toggle: speaker icon with sound waves emanating (on) vs speaker with a slash (off). The morph is a 200ms SVG path interpolation — the same speaker, the waves draw in or the slash slides in. This is the Apple Settings move done in SVG: icon BECOMES the state.

For binary settings without natural iconography (e.g., 'Send weekly digest'), we fall back to a track + handle, but the handle has an inline check or cross icon that morphs — never just a position change.

Persistent change confirmation: for IMPORTANT toggles (anything that affects payments, data deletion, security), we don't show a confirm modal. We show an in-place inline confirmation: the toggle moves to its new state, but immediately below it a small text confirms 'Two-factor auth turned on · 8s to undo' with a subtle countdown bar. Click Undo within 8s to revert. After 8s, the change is committed silently. This is the Gmail 'Undo send' pattern applied to settings.

For reversible/low-stakes toggles, no confirmation — the state change IS the confirmation.

Motion: morph is 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1). Handle slide is the same. Inline confirm: 200ms fade-in + 8s countdown bar that drains linearly. Disappears after 200ms fade-out.

State during async: if the network is slow, the toggle moves to its target state immediately (optimistic) but the morphing icon stays mid-morph until the server acks. On ack: morph completes. On error: morph reverses, an inline error appears below 'Couldn't save — try again'. This is the rare valid use of a 'loading' state inside a control: it's encoded in the animation, not a separate spinner.

Rejected: a 'Save changes' button at the bottom of the settings page. Pure friction. Auto-save is the right default; undo is the right safety net for important settings. Also rejected: toggle that requires confirmation modal for ALL changes. Settings pages with constant modal interruption feel hostile.

design_tokens.colors: Off state: oklch(0.85 0.005 250) bg, oklch(0.55 0.005 250) icon. On state: oklch(0.55 0.16 145) bg, oklch(0.99 0.005 250) icon. Important-change confirmation: oklch(0.96 0.04 145) text + matching countdown bar.
design_tokens.typography: Setting label: Inter 14px regular. Setting description: 13px regular oklch(0.55). Confirmation text: 12px regular.
design_tokens.spacing: Toggle: 44px wide × 24px tall. Icon: 14px. Confirmation text: appears 4px below toggle with 4px gap to next setting (so the layout doesn't shift jarringly).
design_tokens.motion: Icon morph: 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1) SVG path tweening. Handle position: same duration + easing. Confirmation fade-in: 200ms ease-out. Countdown bar: 8000ms linear width transition.

component_architecture: <Toggle<TIcon>> generic over icon component (different icons per setting). Props: { id, label, description, value, onChange, importance?: 'high' | 'normal', iconOn, iconOff }. For high-importance toggles, the parent receives an onChange that's deferred 8s with an inline undo affordance. Uses an internal useToggleConfirmation() hook to manage the deferred commit. SVG icons are custom React components that animate via attribute interpolation.
interaction_details: Click toggle: handle slides to new position over 200ms. Icon inside handle morphs to new state in sync. Background color transitions over 200ms.
High-importance toggle: same visual change, BUT an inline confirmation row appears below with 'X turned on/off · [bar draining] · Undo'. 8 seconds to undo.
Hover toggle: subtle scale shift (1.02) on the handle. Cursor becomes pointer. No other change.
Slow network: handle moves to target immediately, morphing icon stays mid-morph. On server ack: morph completes. On error: morph reverses, error text appears.
Reduced motion: handle position changes instantly, icon swaps without morph, countdown bar shows but doesn't animate (immediate text 'Undo within 8s').
accessibility_considerations: Uses role='switch' aria-checked='true|false' — the correct ARIA pattern for toggles. Connected to label via htmlFor. Description rendered as paragraph after label. High-importance confirmation uses role='status' aria-live='polite' so screen readers announce 'X turned on, Undo within 8 seconds'. Undo is a real button reachable by Tab. Reduced-motion: all animations disabled (morph, slide, countdown bar). Color is supplemented by the icon morph and handle position — the state is perceivable without color. Contrast: handle bg meets 4.5:1 in both states.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) The toggle's icon MORPHS between states (speaker with waves ↔ speaker with slash) — visual state is semantic, not just positional. 2) High-importance toggles use Gmail-style inline undo (8s countdown with drain bar) instead of confirm modals — friction-less safety net. 3) Async pending state encoded in the morph itself (icon stays mid-morph until server acks) — no separate spinner inside a control.

title: Time-series chart with brush-to-zoom, overlap-aware annotations, and event ribbon
design_brief: You're building the performance metrics chart for a backend monitoring tool (think Honeycomb, Grafana, Datadog, Vercel Observability). SREs use this during incidents to correlate metrics with deploys, errors, and config changes. Pain point: most time-series charts dump a line + a few hover tooltips. The bar: Honeycomb's bubble-up + heatmaps, Datadog's annotation ribbon, Vercel Observability's deploy markers.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Zoom via scroll wheel (steals page scroll)
Brush selection that's a transparent overlay that doesn't show what it's selecting
Annotations rendered as floating tooltips on hover only
Spaghetti of overlapping series lines without legend interaction
Hard-coded Y-axis that doesn't auto-fit to brushed range
Generic 'No data' state when a metric is missing
ux_reasoning:
A debugging chart has three concurrent jobs: show the metric trend over time, surface what HAPPENED at any moment (deploys, alerts, errors), and let the operator zoom into a window of interest fast.

Layout: main chart area for the metric line(s). BELOW the main area, a 32px-tall 'context strip' shows the same data zoomed-out for the entire visible time window. Drag-select on the context strip to brush. The main area zooms to the brushed window with axis labels updating, all in 250ms with FLIP-style choreography. This is the canonical 'detail + context' pattern (a.k.a. focus + context) from Tableau, refined for incident workflows.

ABOVE the main area, an 'event ribbon' (24px tall) shows colored vertical bars for events: deploys (blue), alerts (red), config changes (yellow). Hover any event to see its details in a popover. Click an event to zoom the main chart to a window around it (±5min) — events are jump points, not just decorations.

Annotation overlap: when many events cluster, naive rendering stacks them invisibly. We collision-detect and stack overlapping events into a '+N' badge that expands on hover. This is the Datadog approach, with cleaner visual restraint.

Multi-series: when multiple metrics are charted, each gets a distinct color from the OKLCH palette (max 6, beyond that we suggest a different view). Hovering a legend item dims the others to 30% — focus by elimination. Clicking a legend item filters; double-click solos.

Vertical crosshair: hovering the chart shows a thin vertical line at the X position with each series' Y value shown in a top-right legend (not a per-line tooltip — too noisy with 4+ series). Y values are aligned by decimal point (the trading panel trick).

Brush interaction: drag on the context strip to select a range. Drag the handles of an existing brush to resize. Click outside the brush in the context strip to reset to full range. Keyboard: + and - zoom; arrow keys pan.

Rejected: a 'compare to previous period' overlay built-in. Useful but a different mental model; keep it as a separate visualization. Also rejected: rendering all events as full-height vertical lines through the chart. Visual chaos at any density.

design_tokens.colors: Metric line palette (OKLCH, 6 max): same as the funnel palette. Event ribbon: deploy oklch(0.6 0.18 250), alert oklch(0.6 0.18 25), config oklch(0.75 0.16 80). Crosshair: oklch(0.5 0.005 250) at 60% alpha, 1px dashed. Brush: oklch(0.55 0.18 250 / 0.15) fill, 1px solid border in oklch(0.55 0.18 250).
design_tokens.typography: Axis labels: Inter 11px tabular-nums. Y-value legend in top-right: 12px tabular-nums, decimal-aligned. Event popover title: 12px medium. Event timestamp: 11px tabular-nums oklch(0.55).
design_tokens.spacing: Main chart: 280px tall. Event ribbon: 24px. Context strip: 32px. Gap between sections: 8px. Total: 352px.
design_tokens.motion: Brush-to-zoom: 250ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) for axis + data path. Crosshair: instant. Legend hover dim: 100ms ease-out opacity.

component_architecture: <TimeSeriesChart<TSeries extends Series>> generic over series shape. Children: <EventRibbon events={[]} />, <ChartArea series={[]} domain={[xMin, xMax]} />, <ContextStrip series={[]} brush={[]} onBrush={...} />, <Crosshair />, <Legend onHover={...} />. Brush state is derived (full range by default); zooming updates domain prop. No internal layout-changing state for series — that's a parent decision.
interaction_details: Hover main chart: vertical crosshair follows cursor; top-right legend shows each visible series' value at that timestamp.
Brush context strip: drag-select a time window. Main chart zooms with 250ms FLIP — axis labels update, paths redraw.
Click an event marker: main chart zooms to ±5min around that event. Events become navigation, not just decoration.
Click a legend item: toggles visibility of that series. The Y-axis auto-refits to remaining visible series. Double-click: solo.
Overlapping events: collapse into a single colored bar with a '+N' badge; hovering expands to show all events in the cluster as a popover list.
accessibility_considerations: Chart includes a hidden <table> with the underlying data (visible to screen readers, hidden visually) — pure-visual SVG charts are inaccessible without this. Legend items are real <button> with aria-pressed indicating visibility. Event markers are real <button> with aria-label='Deploy at 14:23 — main → v1.4.2'. Keyboard: arrow keys pan, + and - zoom, Esc resets domain. Reduced-motion: brush-to-zoom becomes instant; series opacity transitions become instant. Color: series colors supplemented by the legend names and by hatch patterns on event markers (deploy = solid, alert = striped, config = dotted) for colorblind users.
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Detail + context layout (main chart + brush strip) with FLIP-choreographed zoom transition — operators can scope to a 30s window from a 24h view in one drag. 2) Event ribbon with overlap-collapse means a 200-event deploy storm renders as scannable +N clusters, not visual chaos. 3) Y-value legend in the top-right corner (not per-line tooltips) keeps the chart uncluttered while still showing exact values at any hover position.

title: Y2K-revival music player with chromed gradient chrome and pixelated transport controls
design_brief: You're building a niche music player web app for a vaporwave/internet-archive community (think a modern reimagining of WinAmp's Bento skin + iTunes 1's brushed metal + Microsoft Plus! 2000). Pain point: 'Y2K aesthetic' on the web is usually misapplied: a Comic Sans header on a flat layout doesn't make it Y2K. The bar: Are.na channels documenting 90s-2000s software UI, Frog Design's Trapper Keeper era, real WinAmp screenshots, the visual language of Tony Yotka's early-2000s portfolios.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Comic Sans as the only 'Y2K' signal
Pastel gradients on flat shapes (Memphis design, not Y2K)
Animated GIFs of dancing baby Yoda
Modern flat icons in a Y2K layout (instant pastiche)
Lo-fi pixel fonts at 32px (squanders pixel detail)
No grid — pure scrapbook chaos, mistaking 'fun' for 'design'
ux_reasoning:
Y2K-revival done with rigor: study the actual conventions of 1999-2003 GUI (WinAmp, iTunes 1, Mac OS 9, late-90s Macromedia tools) and apply them with a designer's restraint. Three pillars:

Chrome: real chromed metallic surfaces. NOT a flat gradient — a multi-stop linear gradient (8+ color stops) that simulates a brushed-aluminum reflection. The math: light source at top, dark band 30% from top (the 'horizon line' of the chrome), then a brighter highlight band at 50%, then a darker band at 80%, then a thin specular line at 100%. This was the WinAmp signature. We render via CSS linear-gradient — modern browsers handle 8 stops fine.

Pixel transport controls: play, pause, prev, next as 16×16 pixel-art icons rendered crisply at 32×32 via image-rendering: pixelated. Real bitmap icons in the spirit of WinAmp's classic skin. Custom-drawn, not lifted from a clip-art library.

Typography: a real bitmap font for the LCD-style track display (e.g., 'Silkscreen' or 'Px437 IBM PS/2 thin1' at 11px). Body chrome labels in Tahoma (the OS default of the era) at 11px. The track display LITERALLY looks like a low-res LCD — green pixel font on a dark green-tinted background with a CRT scanline overlay (1px alternating rows at 8% opacity).

Layout: a windowed app with title bar, menu bar, transport, equalizer (a real bar EQ with 16 bands), and playlist. Modular like WinAmp — windows can be moved (drag the title bar) and snap to each other. NOT a single-page modern layout.

Interactions: every button has a 1-frame depressed state (NOT a smooth ease). When you press play, the button goes from raised to depressed instantly, plays for the duration of the click, then returns. This is the haptic of physical hardware.

The taste threshold: every element must serve the period faithfully, but the underlying interaction model must be modern (keyboard accessible, screen-reader navigable). This is design archaeology with modern infrastructure.

Rejected: skeuomorphism for skeuomorphism's sake. The chrome and bitmap font are period-correct; we don't add a fake CD-tray opening animation just because we can. Restraint is what separates revival from cosplay. Also rejected: a CRT bezel around the whole app — would be cosplay; the LCD frame around just the track display is the right scope.

design_tokens.colors: Chrome gradient: linear-gradient(180deg, oklch(0.95 0.005 250) 0%, oklch(0.78 0.005 250) 30%, oklch(0.88 0.005 250) 35%, oklch(0.65 0.005 250) 50%, oklch(0.75 0.005 250) 55%, oklch(0.55 0.005 250) 80%, oklch(0.4 0.005 250) 100%). LCD display: bg oklch(0.18 0.05 145), text oklch(0.85 0.18 145). Window border: 1px outset oklch(0.6 0.005 250). Title bar gradient: linear-gradient(180deg, oklch(0.4 0.08 250) 0%, oklch(0.3 0.1 270) 100%) — the blue Windows-2000 title.
design_tokens.typography: Track display: 'Silkscreen', 'Px437 IBM PS/2 thin1', monospace fallback, 11px. NEVER anti-aliased (font-smoothing: none). Body chrome: Tahoma, 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif, 11px. Title bar: Tahoma 12px bold white.
design_tokens.spacing: Chrome panel padding: 2px (period-correct — pixel-perfect, not modern 16px). Button: 24×16 pixels for transport, 16×16 for window controls.
design_tokens.motion: Button depressed state: instant (1 frame). Window drag: 1:1 finger tracking. EQ bars react to audio: 60Hz update via raf. LCD text scroll (track name longer than display): linear 60s for one cycle.

component_architecture: <MediaPlayer /> renders a <Window /> (chrome wrapper with title bar + content). <TransportPanel /> with bitmap icon buttons. <LcdDisplay /> renders the track info via canvas (for true pixel rendering) or via a bitmap font + scanline overlay. <Equalizer bars={16} levels={audioData} /> renders bars from audio analyser. Windows can be dragged via title bar; positions stored in a context. Keyboard shortcuts (space = play/pause, etc.) are real.
interaction_details: Play/pause: button visually depresses instantly (1 frame), border inverts to communicate the press, returns on click-end.
Track display: bitmap font (Silkscreen) with NO anti-aliasing. Long titles scroll horizontally over 60s (Marquee-style). CRT scanline overlay at 8% alpha gives the LCD look.
Window dragging: title bar is the drag handle. Real positions, no snapping to grid (period-accurate — windows lived where you put them).
Window controls (_  □  ✕): minimize/maximize/close buttons at 16x16 pixels with proper Y2K iconography.
Equalizer (if shown): 16 bars react to live audio data via Web Audio API analyser node, updating at 60Hz.
accessibility_considerations: Every visual button is a real <button> with descriptive aria-label. The LCD display is a real <div> with its text content readable by screen readers — they ignore the bitmap font visual treatment. Pixel icons have aria-hidden because they're decorative; aria-label on the parent button conveys meaning. Reduced-motion: track-name marquee becomes a horizontal scroll-on-hover instead of auto-marquee. Color: chrome and LCD are aesthetic; controls have icon shapes that are perceivable without color. Keyboard: space = play/pause, arrow keys = prev/next, M = mute (period-correct WinAmp shortcuts).
what_makes_this_distinctive: 1) Real 8-stop chrome gradient simulating a brushed-aluminum reflection — the WinAmp signature, not a Memphis pastiche. 2) Bitmap font for the LCD with font-smoothing: none AND a CSS scanline overlay — period-accurate pixel rendering, not vector mimicry. 3) Buttons use border-color inversion (raised → inset) for the depressed state — the Win95/2000 haptic of physical hardware, instantly recognizable.
